content: I remember reading something about bookmakers setting an over/under on the length of the war. What was that line, and how long did the war actually last? This feels like the kind of thing you'd see in Forgotten Realms lore — did it really go that sideways?

Yes — in the Qamis setting the bookmakers in Kard set the over/under for the length of the war at sixty days. In reality the war lasted thirty years. This is a post-war detail from the Qamis era (the present-day campaign after the Ryraxian War).
Thirty years! Wow. In FR that kind of misread usually comes from a deus ex machina like a dragon or a powerful artifact. Was something comparable responsible here?

The notes are explicit that Ryraxos, a bronze dragon, played the decisive role. Ryraxos first delivered Edrad from defeat when things looked bleak, and then proceeded to wage aggressive war across the continent for roughly two and a half decades. Eventually Ryraxos accepted exile to the second moon (Secundus). So yes: a dragon shifted the course of the conflict, and the bookmakers massively underestimated how long that would last. All of this is Qamis-era (post-war) material.
That fits the trope. Is there any more color on Ryraxos himself? In FR dragons vary wildly in competence — is he a cosmic-level threat here or more 'powerful NPC' level?

The notes characterize Ryraxos as bronze and relatively young (perhaps sixty). He is described as formidable and politically consequential, but not invulnerable — "less so than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery." His rise started with his role as an artillery commander. Ultimately he was exiled to Secundus and will not appear in the campaign. That's the full extent of what's in the notes; they explicitly avoid expanding beyond that.
Makes sense. And the bookmakers were in Kard — is that the financial hub? Feels appropriate they'd make the bet. Any consequences for them after the multi-decade bust?

Kard is indeed called out in the notes as a major financial and shipping center; its governance is fragile and much of its population was Edran-speaking and welcomed an invasion twenty-eight years ago. The notes mention the bookmakers in Kard setting the over/under at sixty days, but they do not say whether there were legal or cultural consequences for the bettors after the war dragged on. That detail is a blank space the DM has left open.

Comparing this to Greyhawk or FR, the setting seems to lean into the idea that dragons can upend politics without being omnipotent. Is the campaign tone more 'high-consequence political mess' or 'high-magic dragon-epic'?

From the notes the tone is a mix: dragons are world-shaping (Ryraxos altered borders and politics), but politics and statecraft are central — restoration of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad, fragile balances between Artkos, Edrad, Qis'mos, Rathakos, etc., and lingering problems like drow enclaves. The dragon was pivotal, but the aftermath emphasizes political complexity and messy consequences rather than pure heroic dragon-slaying spectacle. If you prefer FR-style epic dragon campaigns or Greyhawk political intrigues, Qamis supports both angles — but the official notes emphasize political fallout and blank-map hooks as much as magical spectacle.
Last thing: is this definitely Qamis-era stuff? I know there's that Qaringil prequel era too — could that bookmaker line be from that era instead?

This bookmaker anecdote and the thirty-year war are Qamis-era (post-Ryraxian War) details. Qaringil is the prequel set about 200 years earlier on the island city-state of Qaringil; its political map and specifics are different (Rathakos and Acrokos don't exist then). So you should treat the over/under story as modern/Qamis lore, not Qaringil.
My PC is from Forthyr and follows the Dragon Emperor revival sect. Who rules Artkos and what do they want?

Which era do you mean? The setting has two eras in the notes: present-day Qamis (post-Ryraxian war) and the Qaringil prequel (~200 years earlier). Your Forthyr background and the Dragon Emperor revival sect appear in the Qamis (present) notes, so I'll answer for present-day Qamis unless you want the older map.

In present-day Qamis (post-Ryraxian war):
- Artkos is governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth. 
- Politically, Artkos wants to restore its former dominance. The notes say the high kings of Artkos "yearn to restore their dominance over the rest of the Artklan League." They view the new Artklan League (the post-war arrangement) as a pale shadow of the old empire and are interested in regaining influence and authority across the region.
- Artkos includes several exclaves, including a central exclave that contains the city Ath'r (an old imperial seat). It also contains a southwestern enclave centered on Kard, a major financial and shipping center whose governance is fragile.

What the notes do NOT say (important for your PC):
- The notes do not state Artkos's official attitude toward Forthyr or toward the Dragon Emperor revival sect. There is no explicit mention of allies or enemies with Forthyr in regard to the sect.

If you want, I can also summarize the relevant Qaringil-era rulers (about 200 years earlier) for comparison.
Okay — so given I'm a Forthyr believer in the Dragon Emperor revival sect, should I expect Artkos to oppose or tolerate me? Would High King Ezevir Arth act against followers like my PC?

The notes are silent on Artkos's stance toward the Dragon Emperor revival sect, so there is no definitive answer in the campaign documents. That said, only two grounded points can inform likely tensions (but these are suggestions, not stated facts):

- Artkos is explicitly expansionist and wants to restore dominance over the Artklan League. If the Dragon Emperor revival sect in Forthyr is promoting a return of overt dragon rule or mobilizing followers, that could be seen by Artkos as a regional destabilizer and therefore a political threat — a reason Artkos might act against prominent or organized sect activity.

- Conversely, Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, and the notes say the Dragoncaller dynasty gives tacit encouragement to the revival sect. That implies Forthyr has some official protection for adherents inside Forthyr itself, making direct intervention by Artkos more diplomatically fraught unless Artkos wants open conflict with Forthyr.

So, from the notes: there is potential for conflict (Artkos expansionism vs. a Forthyr-backed dragonist movement), but no explicit policy or persecution is recorded. How Artkos actually treats individual followers (harassment, surveillance, open persecution, or indifference) is a blank space the DM can fill. If you want, I can suggest three concrete in-setting hooks the DM might use for your character (e.g., a Kard merchant linked to Artkos intelligence, an Artkos envoy probing Forthyr enclaves, or a local militia suspicious of dragon-worship), or I can summarize Forthyr leadership details from the notes. Which would you prefer?
I missed the session — recap for me: why don't Qaian navigators need a chronometer? I'm a celestial-navigation nerd and want to work through the primsine formula by hand.

Short version: Qaian navigators don't need a chronometer because Primus is geostationary. It is fixed over one point on Qaia (latitude 0, longitude 0) and never moves in the sky for observers on the near hemisphere. That makes longitude a purely geometric problem: measure your latitude from the pole star and measure Primus's elevation above your horizon, then use the primsine function to convert that elevation into the longitude magnitude. The sign (east or west) is resolved by Primus's azimuth (is it east or west of due south). The navigation method and formula are shared across both campaign eras (MOONS.md and NAVIGATION.md).

Key facts you need before doing the math:
- Primus is geostationary at radius r = 42,160 km; Qaia radius R = 6,371 km. Define rho = R/r = 6371 / 42160 = 0.1511.
- Latitude phi is found from the pole star: elevation of the pole star = geographic latitude phi.
- Primus elevation e is measured with your sextant/astrolabe.

Practical procedure:
1) Measure pole star elevation -> phi.
2) Measure Primus elevation e -> look up or compute primsine(e).
3) Use the spherical cosine relation: primsine(e) = cos(phi) * cos(lambda).
4) Solve: cos lambda = primsine(e) / cos(phi); lambda = arccos(...). Determine E/W by noting whether Primus is east or west of due south: if Primus appears east of due south, you are west of 0°; if Primus appears west of due south, you are east of 0°.

Primsine definition (exact):
primsine(e) = rho * cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e))
with rho = 0.1511.

Useful checks: primsine(90°) = 1 (Primus overhead -> sub-Primus point); primsine(0°) = rho (Primus on horizon -> visibility limit). The maximum geocentric angle where Primus is above the horizon is psi_max = arccos(rho) ≈ 81.3°; in practice that means Primus is visible across most of the near hemisphere but not the far side.

If you want, I can walk through a hand calculation now (there's a worked example in NAVIGATION.md). Do you want me to run the numbers with the example values or with your own e and phi?

Yes, please run the worked example from the notes step-by-step so I can follow the arithmetic. Use phi = 30° N and Primus elevation e = 42.2°. Show the primsine calculation and the longitude derivation.

Okay, step-by-step using the exact arithmetic from the notes.

Given: phi = 30°, e = 42.2°, rho = 0.1511.

1) Compute cos(e) and sin(e):
- cos(42.2°) = 0.7408 (from cosine table)
- cos^2(e) = 0.7408^2 = 0.5488
- sin(42.2°) = 0.6717

2) Compute rho * cos^2(e):
- rho * cos^2(e) = 0.1511 * 0.5488 = 0.0829

3) Compute the sqrt term: sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e)). First rho^2 * cos^2(e):
- rho^2 = 0.1511^2 ≈ 0.02283
- rho^2 * cos^2(e) = 0.02283 * 0.5488 ≈ 0.01253
- 1 - that = 0.98747
- sqrt(0.98747) ≈ 0.9937

4) Multiply by sin(e):
- sin(e) * sqrt(...) = 0.6717 * 0.9937 ≈ 0.6675

5) Sum the two parts to get primsine(e):
- primsine(42.2°) = 0.0829 + 0.6675 = 0.7504

6) Now use the longitude formula: primsine(e) = cos(phi) * cos(lambda)
- cos(phi) = cos(30°) = 0.8660
- cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(phi) = 0.7504 / 0.8660 = 0.8666
- lambda = arccos(0.8666) = 30.0° (approximately)

7) Determine E/W sign by azimuth. The worked example says Primus appears slightly to the west of the local meridian, and the rule is: Primus west of due south -> observer is east of the prime meridian. Therefore the observer is at 30° East.

So the final answer: latitude 30° N, longitude 30° E. The arithmetic matches the worked example in the notes.

Want me to show how the primsine values compare to the small lookup table entries (e.g., primsine(45°)=0.837) or to run your own e and phi?

Quick follow-ups: 1) why is parallax important here and how does that differ from using a distant star? 2) what about precision — how accurate can you realistically get with a sextant vs a cross-staff on Qaia?

Good questions.

1) Parallax and why it matters for Primus
- Primus is relatively close (42,160 km) compared with stars, so its apparent position depends on the observer's location on Qaia. That is parallax. Because Primus is not at infinite distance, the elevation you measure encodes both your latitude and your longitude relative to the fixed sub-Primus point. That is precisely why a single elevation measurement yields longitude magnitude once latitude is known.
- Distant stars are effectively at infinite distance, so their apparent direction does not change with observer longitude in any practical way; a star gives you your latitude (via the pole star) but not longitude without timing or another moving object. By contrast, Primus's parallax is large and systematic enough to be used as a geometric longitude reference.
- Note: because Primus is close enough that parallax is significant, converting elevation to geocentric angular distance requires the primsine function (or a precomputed table) rather than a trivial angle subtraction. That is why navigators use the primsine column or compute the formula.

2) Precision expectations (from NAVIGATION.md)
- Instrument precision directly limits latitude and longitude error. The guide gives typical figures:
  - Simple astrolabe (~0.5° measurement) -> latitude ~55 km error; longitude ~55–70 km error
  - Good cross-staff (~0.1°) -> latitude ~11 km; longitude ~11–14 km
  - Careful sextant (~0.05°) -> latitude ~6 km; longitude ~6–7 km
- Sensitivity varies by location: near the equator and prime meridian (Primus overhead) a small elevation error translates to a small longitude error because elevation changes rapidly with longitude there; near the visibility boundary (Primus low on the horizon) the geometry is shallow and longitude becomes less sensitive and more error-prone.

Practical note: navigators on the far-side hemisphere cannot see Primus at all and must fall back on dead reckoning or lunar-distance/star methods — the chronometer-free advantage applies only to the near hemisphere where Primus is visible (visibility limit psi_max ≈ 81.3° from the sub-Primus point).

Want me to compute the expected longitude error for a given e, phi and instrument angular error? I can propagate a small delta-e through the primsine formula and the lambda inversion.
Why don't Rathakos and Acrokos exist in the Qaringil era? That seems weird — in settings like Forgotten Realms you get long-lived kingdoms. What's going on here?

Short answer: because Qaringil is set roughly 200 years earlier than Qamis, and the campaign notes explicitly say Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist in that earlier map. The political map is different: the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r, and the Red Mountain provinces that later become Rathakos and Acrokos are not yet organized into those states.

From the notes:
- Qaringil (the prequel era) lists map differences and explicitly says "Rathakos and Acrokos fully do not exist." 
- In the Qamis present (post-Ryraxian War), Rathakos and Acrokos emerge out of the Red Mountain Republic after Edran occupation and later political shuffling.

If you want more detail, do you mean "why historically in-world they haven't formed yet" (political reasons) or "why the DM chose to leave them off the earlier map" (authorial reason)?
I mean in-world. In-world, why haven't these mountain states formed 200 years earlier? In FR mountains often have old independent duchies and such. Is the Holy Qis Empire just that dominant?

In-world explanation from the notes: yes, central authority is different in the Qaringil era. The Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r and controls central Qamis, Qirkos, and the parts of Qis'mos north of the river. The Red Mountains area is described as "occupied by various non-state peoples" and the empire "claims but does not effectively administer" them. That implies the mountains are politically fragmented and not organized into the later princedoms.

So the key points are:
- The Holy Qis Empire is the major centralized power in that era (Ath'r is the seat). 
- The Red Mountains are not consolidated under a stable state—they are patchworks of non-state peoples, which discourages formation of stable mountain states like Rathakos or Acrokos at that time. 
- Rathakos and Acrokos arise later in the timeline (see the Qamis notes): after the Ryraxian wars, the former mountain provinces were reorganized into the Red Mountain Republic by Edran occupation, and then local politics split that into Rathakos and Acrokos.

The notes do not give deeper ethnographic or geopolitical detail about those non-state peoples, so the exact in-world causes (culture, clan structure, resource distribution, previous conquests) are left as blank spaces the DM can fill.
So the Red Mountain Republic and the Edran occupation are what create Rathakos and Acrokos? That sounds like a later post-war thing. Is that in Qaringil or only in Qamis notes?

Those events are described in the Qamis (present-day) notes, not in Qaringil. Specifically, in the Qamis timeline:
- During/after the Ryraxian War, Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire and Edran forces occupied parts of the mountain provinces and organized them into the Red Mountain Republic.
- Later, when guerrilla resistance and reverses forced an Edran withdrawal, the opposition faction purged the pro-Edran faction and established the new nations of Rathakos and Acrokos.

So the Red Mountain Republic, the Edran occupation, and the split into Rathakos and Acrokos are post-war developments in the Qamis era. They do not belong to the Qaringil era map — which is why those states "fully do not exist" 200 years earlier.

Ah, okay. That's clearer. I keep thinking in FR terms where kingdoms last for centuries. Does the setting explain why the Holy Qis Empire is able to hold central lands more tightly than mountains? Like, why does Ath'r remain the seat?

The notes state that the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r in the Qaringil era and that the emperor is, in theory, elected. They also note that several river cities (like Lock) are "free cities" in cold conflict with the emperor. Beyond that, the docs do not spell out the Empire's administrative strength or specific reasons Ath'r remains the seat.

So we have these established facts from the notes:
- Ath'r is the imperial seat in the Qaringil era.
- The empire controls central Qamis and nearby regions; some cities resist imperial control (free cities). 
- The Red Mountains are a zone of non-state peoples and weak administration.

The detailed reasons why the empire holds the central lands but not the mountains (logistics, roads, economic base, military tradition, cultural cohesion, prior conquests) are not specified in the notes — a deliberate blank space for worldbuilding. If you want, I can offer a few plausible, clearly-labeled hypotheses you could use as a reader or worldbuilder.
Yes, please throw me a few plausible hypotheses but label them as suggestions. Also, a quick aside: you've been saying "Qaringil" and "Qamis" — remind me which is which again?

Quick era refresher (plain):
- Qaringil = the prequel campaign, set roughly 200 years earlier. The island city-state Qaringil is the local focus; the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r in this era. Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist here.
- Qamis = the present-day campaign, post-Ryraxian War. Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus; the Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad; Rathakos and Acrokos exist in this era (they formed after the war).

Suggested hypotheses for why the Holy Qis Empire controls the plains/riverlands but not the Red Mountains (these are optional worldbuilding hooks — NOT in the notes):
1) Logistics and roads: The empire has the river highways and fertile plains under tight control, which lets them project power where supply lines are easy. Mountains are harder to supply and easier for small groups to resist, so they remain semi-autonomous.
2) Economic base: Ath'r and the central lands have tax revenue from agriculture, trade, and river cities, funding professional bureaucries and armies. The mountains lack that steady revenue stream, so imperial governance is weak there.
3) Cultural frontier: The mountain peoples have different social organization (clans, raiding economy, or dragon-cult remnants), making them less likely to be integrated into a centralized imperial bureaucracy.
4) Strategic focus: The empire prizes control of trade routes and agriculturally rich areas; mountains are peripheries that governors deprioritize, leaving them nominally claimed but effectively independent.
5) Historical treaties: There could be long-standing negotiated autonomy with mountain chieftains or religious sites the empire leaves alone (this could explain the "claims but does not effectively administer" language).

If you want I can pick one and sketch a short paragraph of in-world flavor for it you could imagine as canonical, noting it's your/DM's choice to adopt it.

Great. Now, I'm curious — the Qaringil notes mention "blank spaces" a few times. Are the Red Mountains themselves a blank space in Qaringil? Could I reasonably slot in a backstory where Rathakos is an old duchy that later collapsed and got forgotten in the Qamis era? Or would that conflict with the timeline?

The notes treat much of the Red Mountains and surrounding areas as underexplored and deliberately leave blank spaces. Specifically:
- In Qaringil the Red Mountains are "occupied by various non-state peoples" and the empire "claims but does not effectively administer" them. 
- The Qamis notes describe later events that create Rathakos and Acrokos out of the Red Mountain provinces during and after the Ryraxian War.

Because the documents explicitly leave details blank, you could plausibly propose many alternate histories as player-facing worldbuilding — but be careful about direct contradictions with the established timeline:
- A backstory where Rathakos was already an old duchy 200 years before and then collapsed would conflict with the explicit statement that Rathakos and Acrokos "fully do not exist" in the Qaringil era, unless you frame it so the duchy existed even earlier and was replaced by non-state peoples by the time of Qaringil. In other words, you could posit an older polity that fell before the Qaringil snapshot, leaving ruins and memories in the mountains. That would not contradict the notes, provided the duchy is absent by the Qaringil-era map.

The safe moves are:
- Create pre-Qaringil states that have already fallen to make interesting ruins and legends in the Red Mountains. That keeps "Rathakos/Acrokos do not exist in Qaringil" intact.
- Or treat the formation of Rathakos/Acrokos as a  post-Qaringil, wartime political creation (which matches the Qamis notes). 

If you want, I can draft a short ruin/legend vignette that fits neatly into the Qaringil-era blank space without contradicting the established timeline.
Yes, draft that vignette. Keep it short and make sure it's obviously fan-made flavor, not canonical.

Fan-made vignette (not canonical):

Among the shepherds of the lower Red, old stones mark the curves of a ruined road the elders call the King's Way. They point out a crumbled tower on a ridge and tell a single phrase in half-remembered Edran: "Kharon's House stood here." No chronicle in Ath'r mentions Kharon. Pilgrims find a handful of coins stamped with a crown unlike any on current seals and a weathervane carved with a hawk now used as a weather vane in a mountain tavern. 

The tale says a small duchy once lorded over the passes — a polity too minor to matter to Ath'r but rich enough to tempt raiders. It fell sometime before the present age of the Empire: famine, a string of bad harvests, and a quarrel among the lineages opened the passes to bandits; the duchy's citadel burned and its name was scrubbed from imperial ledgers. What remains are broken milestones, a collapsed bridge, and folk songs about a lost lord who may yet return.

Again: this is fan-made color you can use to populate the "blank spaces" in the Red Mountains. It does not appear in the campaign notes and would be a player/MC addition.

Perfect. One last thing — I keep reading 'Q' is pronounced 'Kh'. You're using Qaringil and Qamis in text — how do people actually say those names aloud? Also, is there a standardized language? I'm thinking in FR people might know how to pronounce 'Cormyr' easily.

Pronunciation: the notes say Q is pronounced "Kh" (from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So aloud you would say:
- Qaringil = "Kharingil" (approximate) 
- Qamis = "Khamis" (approximate)

Language and dialect: the setting explicitly has no racial languages. Main national languages are Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas. Old Draconic (Old Qis) is the language of scholarship and treaties. The notes emphasize that these national languages have major regional variations; the "capital" prestige dialect is the default if not otherwise specified. So unlike a single standardized pronunciation system in Forgotten Realms, pronunciations can vary by region and social class, and the Q/Kh rule is the key consistent guide.

Anything else about comparisons to FR or pronunciation you want me to clarify?
evening! quick exec question: what are triple-moon alignments on Qaia and how often do they happen? I'm a tech lead so just hit me with an executive summary first, then we can dig into details.

Executive summary
- Triple-moon alignments refer to near-simultaneous phase alignments of the three inner freely-orbiting moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. They can be near-all-full or near-all-new (i.e., the three moons are all near 100% illumination or all near 0%).
- Frequency: roughly five times per year the three are within 5% of full/new; about once per year they come within 1% of full/new. Very close alignments (within ~0.1%) occur occasionally during multi-year eras when one type (full or new) dominates.
- Cycle behavior: the system goes through multi-year eras where full alignments are common, then a new-moon era, then back. A full cycle between era types is about 12 years.
- Source: this is from the moons system notes (MOONS.md), which is shared across both the Qamis and Qaringil campaigns.
Good. Now explain why they happen at that cadence — what's driving the pattern? Keep it non-mathematical but precise.

Why these alignments happen (precise, non-mathematical)
- Each moon has a different orbital period: Secundus ~4.53 days, Tertius ~8.15 days, Quartus ~29.50 days. Because the periods are different and not simple multiples of each other, their phases slowly drift relative to one another.
- Occasionally the drifting lines up so that all three reach the same phase (all full or all new) at nearly the same time — that is a triple-moon alignment or near-syzygy.
- Because the periods are incommensurate, exact simultaneous alignment is rare, but near-alignments recur with predictable statistics: small offsets (within a few percent) happen several times per year, tighter alignments less often.
- The system has a longer-term modulation: over roughly a 12-year cycle the pattern favors either full alignments or new alignments for several years (a ‘‘full-moon era’’ followed by a ‘‘new-moon era’’). Within an era the best alignments can be extremely tight (notes say some are essentially exact, within 0.1%).
- In short: differing orbital periods produce beat patterns that make near-syzygies fairly common but very tight, exact syzygies only occasional; the multi-year era modulation is a longer beat in that pattern.
Show me concrete examples from the notes — give a couple of specific dates (no need to explain the calendar system) and how close those alignments were.

Examples from the campaign notes (MOONS.md)
- Feb 13, 2253 at 18h PMT — a very close triple-full: Secundus 100.0%, Tertius 100.0%, Quartus 99.8% (listed as the closest triple-full of the decade).
- Apr 26, 2256 at 00h PMT — an extremely close triple-new: Secundus 0.1%, Tertius 0.0%, Quartus 0.2%.
- Sep 21, 2256 at 13h PMT — another very close triple-new: Secundus 0.1%, Tertius 0.2%, Quartus 0.0%.
- May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT — the closest triple-new of the decade: all three within 0.1%.
- May 11, 2259 at 12h PMT — a very close triple-full: Secundus 100.0%, Tertius 99.9%, Quartus 99.8%.
Notes: the percentage columns in the source are the moons' illumination (100% = full, 0% = new). The decade table in the notes shows several alignments within 5% each year and tighter alignments about once per year.
How do triple alignments affect tides and navigation? Short answer first, then a little more detail.

Short answer
- Tides: when Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus align (full or new) their tidal effects add constructively, producing much larger combined oscillating tides than any one moon alone.
- Navigation: the triple alignments themselves don't change the Primus-based longitude method (Primus is geostationary), but the unusual tidal behavior at alignments can matter for coastal pilots and harbour operations.

A bit more detail (still concise)
- Tidal numbers from the notes: Secundus = 1.47× Luna's tidal force, Tertius = 2.04×, Quartus = 0.87×; together their oscillating contribution sums to about 4.4× Luna’s tidal force. Primus contributes a separate static ~2 cm bulge and does not oscillate daily.
- When the three inner moons are simultaneously full or new, their oscillating tidal pulls line up (constructive superposition), so you get the system's largest oscillating tides. Because the moons' periods differ, these high-amplitude tidal events are intermittent and can be predicted from the moons' phases.
- For navigation: Primus remains the immutable longitude anchor (its geostationary position is unchanged). But pilots and coastal operators who depend on predictable tides need to account for these triple-moon high-tide events, which occur during the noted alignments.
You mentioned multi-year eras and a ~12-year cycle. Can you give a quick chart-style summary of what that means and how it showed up in the 2253–2263 sample?

Era summary (compact)
- Definition: The system alternates between eras dominated by frequent triple-full alignments and eras dominated by frequent triple-new alignments. The full/new dominance flips on a multi-year timescale; a complete oscillation between era types is about 12 years.

2253–2263 sample (how the eras appear in the notes)
- 2253–2254: full-moon era (several very close triple-fulls, e.g., 2253-02-13 and 2254-07-02).
- 2255–2258: new-moon era (closer triple-new dates appear, e.g., 2256-04-26 and 2256-09-21; several new alignments listed).
- 2259–2261: back to a full-moon era (notable close fulls like 2259-05-11 and 2259-10-06).
- 2262: moves toward new again (2262-05-24 is the closest triple-new of the decade).

Interpretation: over that ten-year window you can see the multi-year grouping: a short run of full-dominated years, then several new-dominated years, then full again. That is the practical manifestation of the ~12-year modulation described in the moons notes.
Last: if I want to use this in a campaign hook, what are setting-safe ideas I can drop in without inventing new lore not in the notes? Keep them short—3 bullets.

Three setting-safe campaign hooks (grounded in the notes)
- Tide-dependent crisis: a string of triple-full alignments will produce unusually large oscillating tides (totaling ~4.4× Luna’s single-moon effect). Towns, smugglers, and pilots on the coast call in favors or hire adventurers to secure harbors and protect cargo during predicted alignments.
- Religious/ceremonial timing: the Church of Bahamut and other institutions time important rituals or pilgrimages to the rare, very-close triple-full or triple-new events listed in the almanac (use dates like 2253-02-13 or 2256-04-26 from the notes as examples).
- Almanac/astrolabe quest: a navigator or guild needs an up-to-date almanac entry or a damaged primsine table corrected to predict the next tight triple alignment accurately — useful for merchant guilds or naval powers in Qaringil or Qamis.
All three are consistent with the documented moon behavior and with the setting notes (no invented nations/characters beyond those in the source).
Ugh, this setting is already too complicated. Fine — tell me straight: what's the status of half-elves in Edrad and why is it that way?

Short answer: in Edrad the ruling class is explicitly half-elven. The notes say Edrad is ruled by a half-elven nobility, and nobles must be between one quarter and three quarters elf.

Context that matters (Qamis, present-day):
- Edrad is the historically dominant nation, capital Jia on a peninsula, and the Keyberos dynasty has reigned for four centuries (recently restored after the Ryraxian war).
- The half-elven nobility rule is stated as a fact in the setting notes; it determines who counts as Edran nobility.

Why? The notes do not give a canonical origin story or legal history for the rule — they state the requirement but do not explain how or when it was instituted. That is a deliberate blank space the DM has left open.
Okay, so it's just a rule, no backstory? That feels cheap. Are there any hints about how it works in practice — like do commoners get any privilege, or is it only nobles?

The notes are specific about the nobility requirement but silent on broader social details. Concretely:
- Specified: nobility must be 1/4 to 3/4 elf; Edrad is ruled by half-elven nobility.
- Not specified: whether half-elves outside the nobility have legal or social privileges, how strictly blood fractions are verified, marriage laws, or what everyday prejudice or advantages look like.

So those are blank spaces. The DM can (and often will) fill them in during play — good hooks for court politics, genealogical disputes, or rival houses claiming different degrees of "elfness." If you want, I can suggest a few concrete, noncanonical ways a table might handle it (court tests, heraldic disputes, house claims), but I'll mark them as optional ideas, not setting facts.
Yeah give me one quick idea I can steal for a player backstory. But keep it short — I still think this is too fiddly.

Quick hook you can steal:
- Your noble house claims descent from a famed half-elf hero; the house papers show a lineage that reaches the 1/4 elf threshold, but a rival house is digging up a marriage that would push you below it. Your PC grew up managing the family's estates and now has to prove the pedigree (or secure a marriage) to keep the title. 

Note: this uses the canonical rule about the 1/4–3/4 requirement but invents the dispute as a player/GM plot — not a contradiction of the notes.
Does this half-elf rule appear in the Qaringil era too, or is that just a Qamis thing? I'm worried I'll mix eras up.

Good question. The half-elven nobility rule appears in both documents: the Qaringil (prequel, ~200 years earlier) notes also say "Edran nobility is half-elven and must be between one quarter and three quarters elf." So it's presented consistently across eras. When you talk about specific political context, though, be sure to mark the era:
- Qaringil era: Keyberos dynasty had reigned for two centuries (earlier snapshot of the polity).
- Qamis era (present): Keyberos restored, four centuries of dynasty, Ryraxian war just ended — same half-elf rule but different political backdrop.

If you want era-specific implications (like how the recent war changed noble power), ask which era you mean and I will separate the answers.
Alright. One last thing — is the "Q" pronounced like a K or something weird? You mentioned that before and it's bugging me.
Pronunciation note from the setting: Q is pronounced "Kh" (this comes from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So names like Qamis are pronounced roughly "Khamis". Helpful to mention if you want flavor in play.

hey, got a minute? I'm poking into the Bahamut schism and want to know about these "triple-moon alignments" — what are they, how often do they happen, and could they plausibly be a calendar/ritual anchor for rival priests?

Sure — this is astronomical stuff the setting actually spells out, so I can be concrete.

What the triple-moon alignments are
- They refer to the three inner freely-orbiting moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. The phrase means times when all three are simultaneously very near full or very near new (i.e., near syzygy). This is a real recurring feature of the moon system on Qaia.
- The moons involved and a few physical notes (shared across both Qaringil and Qamis eras): Secundus (retrograde, ~4.53 d), Tertius (prograde, ~8.15 d — basis for the ~8-day week), Quartus (the large, monthlike moon, ~29.5 d).

How often they happen (from the setting notes)
- The three inner moons are simultaneously within 5% of full or new about five times per year.
- They are simultaneously within 1% about once per year.
- There is a longer multi-year cycle: eras alternate between “full-moon eras” (when the frequent alignments are all-full) and “new-moon eras” (when they are all-new). That full cycle is roughly 12 years.
- The notes give concrete examples for the decade 2253–2263 (PMT times): e.g. Feb 13, 2253 at 18h PMT is an especially close triple-full; May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT is the closest triple-new of that decade (all three within 0.1%). Those examples show how exact alignments can be.

Physical consequences the notes do record
- Tidal load: the oscillating tidal drivers (Secundus + Tertius + Quartus) sum to about 4.4 times the tidal force of Luna on Earth. That means when the three line up (new or full), their tidal effects reinforce and you should expect a noticeably stronger combined tide than from any one moon alone. Primus is a separate case and only produces a small static bulge.

About ritual or calendar use (what the notes permit vs. what they do not)
- The campaign notes do not record any explicit religious significance attached to the triple alignments or any institutional practice by the Church of Bahamut tied to them. In other words: the setting is silent on whether Bahamut priests treat these alignments as sacred or politically useful.
- That silence is a deliberate blank space the DM can fill. Given the regularity and predictability of the alignments (five-ish events per year at 5% tolerance; ~once per year at 1% tolerance; a ~12-year era cycle), they make a natural, obvious anchor for liturgy, fasts, blessings, or political theater. They also produce visible, sometimes dramatic changes in night brightness and tides, which are the kind of observable phenomena religious institutions historically latch onto.

If you're thinking about the schism specifically
- What the notes do say about the Qamis-era Bahamut situation: the Church of Bahamut is very influential in Qis’mos; Qama is the traditional seat but was occupied by drow late in the war, who installed a drow high priest (Qe'lth IV). An alternate candidate (Qachu II) operates from Qis without the relics of office. That is the schism noted in the setting.
- Nothing here ties those political/religious rivalries to moon alignments explicitly. So whether a faction uses a triple-full or triple-new as a show of legitimacy, a time for mass ordination, or to schedule a public excommunication is up to you/the DM.

Want hooks I can suggest (clearly hypothetical) for a Bahamut-schism-minded character?
- The drow-installed clergy in Qama stage a major liturgy timed to a triple-full (bright sky, dramatic public rites) to demonstrate control of public spectacle.
- The rival claimant in Qis holds secret ordinations at the rare triple-new (dark nights, easier secrecy) and claims a prophecy tied to darkness and renewal.
- A coastal diocese times a naval blessing to a close triple-syzygy because tides will be strongest and sailors will notice the difference.

Do you want one or two ritual/sermon outlines or an in-world diary entry (short) showing how a Qis priest might spin an alignment into propaganda? I can keep it strictly speculative and labeled that way.

That was great — yes please, give me a short sermon snippet a Qis priest might give during a triple-full alignment that would appeal to conservative Bahamut followers in Qama, and one the rival claimant might preach at a triple-new to appeal to reformists. Keep them short and punchy.

Got it — two labelled, brief, in-setting sermon snippets. Note: these are imaginative hooks; the setting notes do not record actual Bahamut liturgy tied to moons.

Sermon for a triple-full (for conservative Bahamut followers in Qama)
"Look up, children of the Light. The three heavens have conspired this night to crown our city with perfect flame. Secundus, Tertius, Quartus — all full, all in witness. The dragons once ruled by force; our duty is to rule by right. The brightness you see is not chance: it is a sign that the old order endures. Stand with Qama. Uphold the rites, honor the relics, and remember: when the heavens align, so must men with their oaths. Those who would tear down the temple do so in daytime; it is at night like this that we remind the world who holds the true light." 

Sermon for a triple-new (for the rival claimant in Qis)
"Brothers and sisters, in the deepest dark the stars speak truest. Tonight Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are gone from sight together — a clean silence. Darkness is not emptiness but a place of renewal. Old relics that sit cold in a stolen city cannot warm our hearts. In the new night we plant the seed of the changed Church: mercy for the hungry, judgment for the corrupt, and new rites for a people who remember why they followed Bahamut in the first place. Let the hidden light of our conviction be the lantern that guides us until morning." 

Want these expanded into a short ritual or an in-character handout to give to players?

Hi — I'm a linguistics hobbyist and curious about two things at once: what exactly are the Southern Reaches (I saw the name in the Rathakos blurb) and why are they described as unstable? Also I keep seeing Q in names — how does that Q/Kh thing and Old Draconic fit into place names like Southern Reaches' towns?

Good questions — I’ll keep era-clear: you mean the Qamis present-day setting (post-Ryraxian War).

What the Southern Reaches are (what the notes say)
- The Southern Reaches are part of Rathakos in the present-day Qamis campaign. They were annexed from Edrad during the recent upheavals. 
- Rathakos itself is a mountainous border state along the Red Mountains; the campaign is expected to start there.

Why they are unstable (explicit facts from the notes)
- The notes say Rathakos “already suffers from internal unrest in many places, especially in the Southern Reaches annexed from Edrad.”
- Contributing political facts from the notes: Rathakos was made a princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r but has no prince; it is governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council deliberates on a monarch. That weak or provisional government helps instability.
- The Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored; mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults are another source of unrest in the region generally (this is called out in the notes).

What the notes do not specify (be honest)
- The notes do not give town names, exact demographics, precise grievances, landholding patterns, economic resources, or which factions are active in the Southern Reaches. Those are blank spaces the DM intentionally left open.

So, in short: the Southern Reaches are an annexed border region of Rathakos whose instability is explicitly tied to recent annexation from Edrad, provisional governance (Regent John Dunne, no prince yet), and nearby mountain communities with lingering dragon-cult loyalties. The fine-grained local details are blank and available as hooks for play.

Okay — can you say more about likely social and linguistic features there, based only on the notes? For example: would the population be Edran-speaking because it was annexed from Edrad? Would dragon-cult communities speak differently? I don't want you to invent new nations, just work from what's given.

Stick strictly to the notes, here are the constrained inferences and explicit statements:

What the notes give us that relates to language and population
- The Southern Reaches were annexed from Edrad, so it is reasonable (from the notes) to expect a strong Edran cultural and linguistic influence — the notes explicitly mention Edran-speaking areas elsewhere (for example, Kard has many Edran-speakers). The notes do not list exact census numbers or proportions for the Southern Reaches.
- The notes repeatedly state that there are national languages (Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas) and that languages are not racial; they have major regional variation. Without added detail, assume a prestige capital dialect plus regional variants.

Dragon-cult mountain communities (what the notes say)
- The notes state some mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults. That is given as a source of unrest. The notes do not say how those communities speak or whether they preserve Old Draconic forms.

What the notes do NOT specify (so avoid inventing):
- The notes do not specify whether the Southern Reaches are majority Edran-speaking, bilingual, or otherwise; they do not list local place names, specific dialectal features, or whether dragon-cult groups have a distinct sociolect.

So: from the notes you can fairly expect Edran influence in the annexed territory and regional dialectal variation. Whether dragon-cult communities keep archaic Old Draconic features is not stated; that is left as a blank the DM can fill in.

Right — tell me about the Q/Kh pronunciation and Old Draconic. The notes say Q is pronounced Kh and that Old Draconic (Old Qis) is the language of scholarship. How would that show up in place names or official documents in the Southern Reaches, given the political back-and-forth with Edrad?

Again: staying strictly to the notes and distinguishing canonical facts from open possibilities.

Canonical points from the notes
- The setting explicitly states: Q is pronounced "Kh" (this comes from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So any canonical guidance: names with Q are intended to be pronounced with a Kh sound.
- Old Draconic (or Old Qis) is the language of scholarly publications and treaties. The notes also say the "q without u" that appears in many place names is from Old Draconic.
- National languages are the main vernaculars; Old Draconic is the learned/official register used in diplomacy and scholarship.

How that would plausibly manifest in names and documents (staying within the notes)
- Place names in official treaties and scholastic publications will use Old Draconic orthography and the Q-letter forms (the notes explicitly say this is where the "q without u" comes from). The canonical pronunciation guidance is that Q is pronounced "Kh." So any official documents or scholarly maps that use Old Draconic spelling would preserve Q (pronounced Kh) in names.
- The notes emphasize regional variation in national languages. So a local Edran-speaking population in the Southern Reaches might pronounce or adapt Old Draconic names into their local dialect, but the notes do not say exactly how. The only fixed rule in the lore is that Q is pronounced "Kh" in the setting's intended pronunciation.

What the notes leave blank (important for a linguist/player)
- The notes do not specify whether the annexed Southern Reaches keep Old Draconic forms in everyday speech, whether local officialdom insists on Old Draconic orthography for legal texts, or whether Edran administrations replace Old Draconic forms with Edranized spellings. Those are DM/party-level choices.

If you want to build a consistent local flavor without contradicting the notes: treat Old Draconic as the formal written register (treaties, academy), pronounce Q as Kh when reading Old Draconic forms, and let local dialectal variation determine whether spoken place names in the Southern Reaches sound closer to Edran or retain an archaisms from Old Draconic. The notes support either possibility, but they do not mandate one.

Nice. One more deep question: could the dragon-cult mountain communities be a conservative refuge for Old Draconic forms or would the notes suggest otherwise? In other words, is there anything that argues for or against archaic linguistic retention among those cults?

The notes give some relevant signals but no definitive answer; I will separate what they say from what they leave open.

Relevant signals in the notes
- The notes explicitly link the mountain communities to "old dragon cults." That phrase implies cultural continuity with older religious traditions tied to dragons.
- Old Draconic (Old Qis) is the language of scholarship and treaties; it is presented as an older, prestigious tongue.

What follows plausibly from those two points (within the notes' bounds)
- Cultural groups that emphasize continuity with the past — e.g., "old dragon cults" — are natural candidates to preserve archaic rituals, terminology, and possibly linguistic forms in ritual contexts. The notes do not state this directly, but the pairing (old cults + an older prestige language) makes the idea plausible and consistent with the material.

What the notes do not provide (so this is explicitly blank)
- The notes do not say whether dragon-cult communities actually use Old Draconic in ritual speech, nor whether they preserve phonology, morphology, or writing conventions of Old Draconic. There is no canonical list of dragon-cult liturgy, no vocabulary examples, and no sociolinguistic surveys.

Conclusion tied to the setting rules
- Nothing in the notes contradicts the idea that dragon-cult communities might conserve Old Draconic forms in ritual or special registers. The notes also do not confirm it. This is one of the intentional blank spaces the DM left open and is a good place for a player or GM to add local color.

If you want a player-safe, setting-consistent option: give cults an archaic ceremonial register using Old Draconic orthography/pronunciation (Q = Kh) while letting everyday speech remain a regional dialect of the national language (likely Edran in the annexed Southern Reaches). That follows the explicit facts (Old Draconic as prestige language; regional dialect variation; dragon cults exist) without inventing any prohibited specifics.

hey — quick question for my campaign prep. which of Qaia's moons are prograde vs retrograde? i swear i read somewhere that Secundus was prograde and Tertius retrograde but i might be mixing up the prequel stuff. also, any obvious game-mechanical hooks (tides, weeks, navigation) i should know about?

Good catch to double-check — and nice instinct about era confusion. The moon system (MOONS.md / NAVIGATION.md) is shared across both the Qamis present and the Qaringil prequel, so the orbital directions are the same in both eras. You might be mixing up names, so here is the canonical list from the notes:

- Primus: geostationary, labeled “prograde (magically anchored).” It is effectively fixed in the sky above the equator (never rises or sets) — a permanent, motionless point.
- Secundus: retrograde.
- Tertius: prograde.
- Quartus: prograde.
- Sextus: retrograde.
- Septimus: retrograde.

(Quintus is a trace particle at Qaia’s Sun–Qaia L4 and isn’t a normal moon.)

A couple of observational/physical details that matter for play and navigation (from the setting notes):
- Retrograde moons (Secundus, Sextus, Septimus) rise in the west. The notes explicitly say an observer on the near side sees Primus fixed, Secundus sweeping east-to-west (retrograde sense), while Tertius and Quartus move west-to-east (prograde).
- Primus is only visible from the near-side hemisphere; the far side never sees it. Primus is also the prime-meridian reference used for celestial longitude (no chronometer required).
- Tidal effects: the oscillating tidal load on Qaia is dominated by Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Their tidal contributions are 1.47× (Secundus), 2.04× (Tertius), and 0.87× (Quartus) Luna — combined ≈ 4.4× Luna. Primus contributes a permanent static ~2 cm bulge but no oscillating tide.
- Periods you can use mechanically: Secundus ≈ 4.53 days, Tertius ≈ 8.15 days (Tertius is the basis for the Qaian week), Quartus ≈ 29.5 days (the “monthly” body). Those are explicit in the notes.

If you want game-mechanical hooks, the notes don't define rules, but they give firm facts you can base houserules on. A few straightforward, faithful ideas:
- Tide mechanics: make coastal hazards more complex than a single daily tide — use multiple overlapping cycles (use the three dominant frequencies listed in the notes) to create unpredictable high/low timings for certain encounters or traps.
- Moonrise flavor: retrograde moons rising in the west is a clear, repeatable visual cue — useful for navigation checks, omen interpretation, or timing rituals that require “moonrise in the west.”
- Calendar/rituals: Tertius gives an 8-day week in-setting; Quartus gives a monthly rhythm. Triple-moon alignment windows (the notes show these happen multiple times a year and have especially close alignments once per year) are excellent campaign events — the notes even include a sample decade of close alignments you can hotlink to if you want exact dates for a particular era.
- Navigation: Primus being geostationary is a major in-world navigation advantage on the near side; far-side characters lack it, which is a clear skill/challenge split for player groups.

If you want, I can: (a) output a compact table you can paste into your GM notes, (b) sketch a simple houserule for tides using the three oscillation periods, or (c) pick a triple-alignment date from the decade in the notes and convert it into an adventure hook. Which would help most?

nice — do the retrograde/prograde labels change between Qamis and Qaringil eras? also i might run a werewolf-style curse keyed to 'moon phase direction' — is there anything about a moon's retrograde motion that would mechanically reverse a phase (like full->new) or is that nonsense?

They do not change between eras. The moon system and its orbital properties (prograde/retrograde status, periods, tidal numbers, Primus geostationary behavior, etc.) are shared across Qamis (present) and Qaringil (prequel). So you can rely on the same directions and periods in either era.

About your werewolf-style idea: the notes do not define any magical rules tying creatures to orbital direction, nor do they say that retrograde motion reverses a moon's phase. Phases (full/new) are a function of Sun–moon–Qaia geometry and are unchanged by whether a moon orbits prograde or retrograde. In other words, retrograde motion changes the apparent direction of travel across the sky (and the local time of rising/setting), but it does not intrinsically flip full to new or swap waxing/waning.

Concrete points you can use without inventing new astronomy:
- Retrograde moons still have normal phases. Secundus can be full or new like any other moon; its retrograde orbit just means it moves opposite the prograde moons in the sky and rises in the west.
- Because the three main oscillating moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) have different periods and are incommensurate, their phases line up in complex ways. The setting notes say they are simultaneously near-full or near-new several times per year, and within very tight tolerances (~0.1%) about once per year. That gives you deterministic, rare alignment events to tie powerful curses, rites, or transformations to — without inventing a rule that retrograde equals reversed phase.
- Flavor houserule option (explicitly homebrew): you could make curses sensitive to apparent direction of motion as a ritual condition — for instance, "target undergoes transformation only when Secundus is rising in the west over the target's horizon" — that is fully consistent with the notes because Secundus does rise in the west. Just be explicit to players that this is a houserule and not a canonical astronomical effect.

If you want, I can translate the triple-alignment calendar entries from the notes into sample in-game dates for your timeline (Qamis-era year numbering is in the notes for 2253–2263), or propose a simple mechanical rule for tide/timing-based transformations that uses the documented moon periods rather than inventing new orbital behavior. Which do you prefer?

give me a simple houserule for tides that uses the documented periods. i want something crunchy but not math-heavy at the table — players should be able to eyeball when high/low will be roughly happening.

Okay — here's a compact, table-friendly houserule built directly from the documented moons and designed to be easy to use at the table. It is explicitly a houserule (the setting notes give the periods and that the oscillating load is dominated by Secundus, Tertius, Quartus; they do not give a game rule).

Tide Houserule (simple, table-friendly)
1) Key inputs (use the setting periods):
   - Secundus cycle: 4.5 days (round 4.53 -> 4.5 for ease)
   - Tertius cycle: 8 days (8.15 -> 8)
   - Quartus cycle: 29.5 days (round to 30)
2) Base tide level: pick “average” for scene start.
3) Each cycle contributes a slow oscillation that nudges tide one step up or down. Treat each as a separate 3-state oscillator: Low / Normal / High.
   - Secundus: strong (weight 2)
   - Tertius: strongest (weight 3)
   - Quartus: moderate (weight 1)
   (Weights reflect the relative tidal numbers in the notes: 1.47, 2.04, 0.87 respectively.)
4) Determine the combined tide state by summing weights for moons currently in High minus weights for moons currently in Low. Interpret the sum:
   - Sum <= -3: Very Low tide
   - -2 to -1: Low tide
   - 0 to +1: Normal tide
   - +2 to +3: High tide
   - >= +4: Very High tide
5) How to eyeball the moons at the table without math:
   - Mark the current phase/position for each of the three moons with a simple token: High if near local high tide phase for that moon (roughly when that moon is on the local meridian opposite the sun), Low if roughly opposite. If you prefer, let players make a Perception or Navigation check to get a more precise local estimate.
   - Secundus flips its token every ~4.5 days, Tertius every ~8 days, Quartus every ~30 days.
6) Play consequences (examples):
   - Very High tide: shores are submerged; coastal terrain is slippery; some shipboard maneuvers are easier/harder. Unsecured moored boats roll and take small damage unless secured (DM call).
   - Very Low tide: exposed rocks and mudflats; enemies may be trapped on shoals; some normally-submerged routes become drivable.

Why this stays faithful: the rule uses only the documented relative strengths and documented periods (rounded for table speed). It preserves complex overlapping cycles (because the three oscillators combine), but gives a simple three-state mental model for each moon so you and the players can eyeball trends without calculations.

If you want, I can give a one-page quick reference card you can hand the players (tokens, flip schedule, simple examples) or convert the weights/thresholds into a small d6 roll table so the DM can resolve tide state with one die instead of summing weights.
hi nanochat — weekend DM here, quick executive question: what's the difference between the Silk/Wool guilds and the Mercantile Guild in Qaringil? Keep it short and then build up a bit.

Short executive summary (Qaringil era, ~200 years before the Qamis campaign):
- Silk and Wool are product-specific guilds that handle both production and trade of their respective goods (raw material, manufacture, and sale).
- The Mercantile Guild is the general merchants' guild that regulates trade in other commodities and broader commercial activity beyond specialized textiles.

Want me to expand into who controls them, political influence, and player-facing hooks?
Yes — expand. Who runs them and how do they interact politically in the city?

Expanded (still Qaringil city-state material):
- Leadership and families: the setting lists specific families tied to guilds. Vitelli leads the Silk Guild. Manos is involved in both Silk and Wool. The Mercantile Guild leadership was held by Qanto until they lost it to a minor gnomish family aligned with the Exarch; the notes identify that gnomish family as Tiddwar, who now leads the Mercantile Guild. Other families: Peixota leads Shipping and is also involved in mercantile and judicial business; Volmer leads Banking and is involved in shipping; Qanto remains influential in judicial and banking matters.
- Political role: silk and wool guilds are vertically integrated in the notes — they cover production and trade — so they control supply chains for those textiles within the city. The Mercantile Guild is the umbrella for general commerce and "other commodities," meaning it sets norms and likely arbitration for cross-commodity trade, overseas deals, and non-textile markets.
- Power dynamics: that overlap (Silk/Wool doing trade vs Mercantile regulating trade) is a natural source of friction. The Five Families and the guild leadership are intertwined: guild control equals political and economic leverage in Qaringil's republic-style government. The Exarch and the alignment of minor families (like Tiddwar) matter for who controls the Mercantile agenda.

If you want, I can lay out concrete ways that friction shows up in the city (tax fights, port priority, favored contracts) or give a short list of adventure hooks tied to those tensions.
Give me three tight player-facing hooks grounded in the notes — no invented NPC names.

Three player-facing hooks (grounded in the Qaringil notes; no new NPC names):
1) Supply-chain sabotage: a Wool mill's shipments keep disappearing en route to the docks. Silk and Wool guild members accuse Mercantile shippers of skimming cargo; the Mercantile Guild denies it and points to a competing family's agents. Players are hired (or pressured) to investigate, recover goods, and choose whether to expose the guilty party or broker a quiet settlement.
2) Guild leadership dispute: Qanto recently lost control of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family aligned with the Exarch. Old Mercantile allies worry about changing trade terms that would favor the Exarch's clients. Players are asked to gather evidence of back-room deals (ledgers, contracts) and decide whether to leak it to a rival family, hand it to the Judicial Guild, or use it as leverage.
3) Port priority and shipping lanes: the Shipping Guild (led by Peixota) wants priority access to a new bridge or quay maintenance contract; Silk merchants need faster transit for perishable dyes and fine textiles. The Mercantile Guild is adjudicating access. Players can serve as neutral consultants, smuggle alternate cargo to prove a point, or sabotage the competing berth to swing the ruling.

If you want each hook converted into a one-session beat map (conflicts, skill checks, likely outcomes), I can do that next.
How mechanically feasible is it for the party to join one of these guilds? Any rules notes in the docs?

Mechanically feasible in the setting: yes. The Qaringil notes explicitly say the party (fifth level in the prequel writeup) can be full guild members of anything relevant to their class.

What the notes do not specify (blank space):
- Exact membership requirements (fees, trials, ranks) for each guild.
- Mechanical bonuses or official in-setting benefits tied to membership (discounts, legal immunities, ranked favors).
- Formal enforcement mechanisms, tariffs, or the Judicial Guild's procedural details.

Practical table-ready approach (DM-friendly, not from the notes): you can treat membership as granting access to guild facilities, introductions to patron families, and a steady stream of guild-related missions. If you want, I can propose a compact, rules-light membership framework you can drop into your campaign.
Yes, give a short, droppable membership framework (no lore additions). Keep it system-agnostic and minimal.

Minimal, system-agnostic membership framework (drop-in):
- Entry: membership requires demonstration of relevant skill (one contested task, or presentation of credentials) and payment of a modest initiation fee. Alternatively, sponsorship by an existing member substitutes for fee or task.
- Benefits (narrative): access to guild facilities, official introductions to allied merchants/families, first call on guild jobs, and informal dispute mediation through guild contacts.
- Obligations: members accept reasonable guild jobs, must not openly sabotage guild interests, and owe a small periodic dues or percentage cut on guild-facilitated contracts.
- Enforcement: violations trigger guild arbitration; severe breaches can result in fines, temporary suspension, or being "unendorsed" (loss of introductions). Criminal acts go to the city's judicial process.

The notes leave exact numbers and mechanics blank; pick thresholds that fit your table (e.g., fee = 10% of a character's cash, dues = minor weekly cost, arbitration = roleplay + social checks). Want me to convert that into a couple of sample checks and DCs for a typical 5e party?
Yes, give a couple quick 5e-style examples for initiation task and arbitration resolution.

hey, got a minute for a setting question — I'm thinking of running the Qaringil prequel as a one-shot and I'm building a PC. What's the cultural role of Jia in Qamis (Qaringil era)? I want local color for a capital-born character.

Quick clarifier: you asked about Jia in the Qaringil prequel era (~200 years earlier)? I can answer for that era specifically (the notes also mention Jia in the later Qamis era, but some details differ). Which do you want? If prequel, I'll stick to Qaringil-era facts.
Yes, Qaringil-era — that's the one-shot. Go ahead.

Okay — Qaringil-era summary (facts from the notes):

- Jia is the coastal capital of Edrad and sits on a peninsula jutting into the Central Sea. The city is described as by far the most culturally influential city in Qamis.  
- Edrad is the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis at this time. The Keyberos dynasty has ruled Edrad for two centuries in the Qaringil-era notes.  
- Edran nobility is explicitly half-elven and must be between one quarter and three quarters elf (that rule is stated in the notes).  
- Languages: the main national languages exist (Edran, Artklan, Qis, etc.). Capital dialects are the prestige forms; the notes say to assume you can speak the capital dialect and one regional dialect.  

What the notes do NOT specify about Jia (blank spaces you can fill): neighborhood names, specific guilds or markets in Jia, street-level demographics, festivals, architecture styles, precise customs of the half-elven court, and so on. Those details are left open by the DM.

Nice. Give me practical local color I can drop into roleplay — how does Jia feel on the street? What kinds of people and smells and sounds? Be explicit about which bits are from the notes and which are my (or your) one-shot seeds.

From the notes (hard facts):
- Jia = coastal capital on a peninsula in the Central Sea.  
- It's the culturally dominant city for Edrad and the seat of half-elven nobility (noble-blood requirement quoted above).  

Seeds you can use for local color (NOT in the notes — optional one-shot material):
- Port bustle: a wide central harbor with merchant quays, rope and tar smells, fish smokehouses, cargo crews shouting in several national dialects (Edran, merchants from Artkos, Islas sailors). Use this to emphasize Jia's cultural reach.  
- Courtly contrast: a polished palace district of half-elven nobles in layered silks and formal etiquette just uphill from rough dockside neighborhoods. Nobles speak the prestige Edran dialect; dockworkers use regional variants.  
- Street sounds: boat horns at dawn, market hawkers calling spices and salted fish, distant training at military barracks (Edrad is dominant), priests or scribes reading proclamations in Old Qis at formal events.  
- Small details to drop in: ink-stained apprentices carrying ledgers, banners with the Keyberos sigil along official avenues, tavern songs mixing languages.  

If you want a single sensory line for a PC to notice on arrival: "The harbor fog lifts to reveal pennants from half a dozen ports; a procession of officious clerks in knife-edged Edran robes passes between a fishwife arguing about price and a merchant counting coin in a shaded doorway."  

All of the above beyond the bullets labeled “From the notes” are optional flavor you can adopt for your one-shot; the original docs leave them unspecified.

About that half-elven nobility rule — "must be between one quarter and three quarters elf." Is enforcement described anywhere? Could my PC be a lowborn noble with only a little elf blood and still claim title?

The notes state the rule verbatim: "Edran nobility is half-elven and must be between one quarter and three quarters elf." The notes do not describe how that is enforced (no details about heralds, courts, testing, or social practice).

Options you can choose as DM for the one-shot (explicitly not in the notes; pick one):
- Legal/ceremonial enforcement: the city keeps genealogical rolls and proof of blood is required for certain privileges. That creates plot hooks (forged pedigrees, a registry clerk, a ruined branch of a house).  
- Social enforcement: the rule is a strong social norm rather than hard law — background checks happen via reputation and witnesses; a PC with dubious blood might be tolerated but snubbed in court.  
- Flexible/competitive enforcement: powerful houses accept exceptions for money or alliances; a lowborn with some elf ancestry could buy a title or be knighted into a lesser house, but rivals will use blood as a smear.  

For a one-shot, the second or third option gives you immediate roleplay friction without complex bureaucracy. If you want mechanical stakes, tie an NPC (a genealogist, a proud minor noble, or a tax official) to the enforcement method you pick.
Good. Languages: if my PC is from Jia, what languages would they plausibly speak at start? And how do I justify a bonus language?

From the notes (rules about language):
- There are no racial languages.  
- Q is pronounced "Kh" (Old Draconic influence).  
- The main languages are national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas.  
- Languages are not standardized; capital dialects are the prestige forms.  
- "For your starting language, assume you can speak the capital dialect and one regional dialect."  
- "I guess everyone gets one bonus language too but you have to tell me how you know it."  

So a Jia-born PC should certainly speak the Edran capital dialect. Pick one regional dialect as their second language (this can be from a border province, an island dialect, etc.). For the bonus language, the notes require you to justify it in backstory. Plausible justifications (player choices, not in notes):
- Family trade: grew up in a merchant household that did business with Islas or Artkos, so you learned Islas or Artklan.  
- Education: private tutors versed in Old Qis (scholarly language) or a foreign tutor from Forthyr.  
- Service: served on a ship trading to Kard or Qaringil and picked up a dialect.  

Pick whichever fits the PC concept; the official rule is simply: you start with the capital dialect + one regional dialect, and you must explain any bonus language in your backstory.
Are there any big political tensions in the Qaringil-era that would affect someone from Jia? Factions to pick as enemies or allies?

Facts from the notes that bear on politics in the Qaringil era:
- Edrad is the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis.  
- The Keyberos dynasty has reigned for two centuries in this era.  
- The Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r over central Qamis (it is powerful in the region).  
- Artkos is only its historical heartland in the northwest at this time (less dominant than later).  
- The Red Mountains are occupied by various non-state peoples and are claimed but not effectively administered.  

The notes do not list specific faction names inside Jia (no house-by-house rivalries for Jia are provided), nor specific recent political crises centered on Jia in the prequel notes — those are blank spaces.  

Useful tensions you can lean on (these are DM/PC seeds, not stated facts):
- Edrad vs. the Holy Qis Empire over influence in central Qamis (good for espionage or diplomatic missions).  
- Merchant interests vs. noble prerogative in the capital — merchants want lower tariffs, nobles want control (fits a capital-born merchant's child).  
- Border pressure from the unsettled Red Mountains — veterans, refugees, and frontier lords bring conflict into the capital's politics.  

All of the above are plausible given the notes, but the specifics (which houses back which policy, who runs Jia's courts, local guild influence) are left for you to define.
One last thing — can you give me a one-paragraph starter hook set in Jia for a one-shot (label it as optional flavor)? Keep it short.

Optional one-shot starter hook (seed, not in the notes):

"At dawn the Keyberos banners are draped in mourning—an obscure cousin of the ruling house has died and the city clerk claims the title will be inherited by a contested branch; the registry clerk who can prove the line has vanished overnight. The players are hired—quietly, and with coin from a worried merchant faction—to recover the clerk's ledger from a dockside thieves' den before the mourning week ends and the rival house moves to claim the inheritance."

This is purely a suggested hook you can use to showcase court politics, Jia's port character, and the half-elven blood-rule as potential leverage.
hi, qamis — I heard Primus is used to find longitude. Why cant people on the "far side" of Qaia use Primus to navigate?

Good question — this comes straight from the moons/navigation notes (these moon facts are shared for both the Qamis and Qaringil eras). Short answer: Primus is permanently invisible from the far-side hemisphere, so you cannot measure its elevation there and therefore cannot use the Primus elevation method to get longitude.

A bit more detail, in plain talk:
- Primus is truly geostationary: it sits over one fixed point on Qaia (the sub-Primus point at 0°N, 0°E) and does not move across the sky. That means people on the near side always see the same fixed point; people on the opposite hemisphere never see it because it is forever below their horizon.
- The navigation method that uses Primus needs you to measure Primus's elevation above your horizon. That elevation, combined with your latitude (from the pole star), gives you longitude via the primsine lookup/formula in the NAVIGATION notes. If Primus is below the horizon you have nothing to measure.
- Visibility has a hard geometric limit: Primus is visible out to a geocentric angle of about 81.3° from the sub-Primus point (so at the equator that corresponds to roughly ±81° longitude). That range narrows at higher latitudes. Beyond that boundary Primus is permanently hidden.

So the far-side simply lacks line-of-sight to Primus. No amount of waiting or clever timing helps — Primus never rises there.

If you want, I can explain how far-side navigators cope (notes mention dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, and lunar-distance methods using Secundus or Quartus), or walk through the primsine idea step by step.
Okay, what exactly do you mean by "far side"? And for folks who can see Primus — does it really never set or move? I'm trying to picture it.

"Far side" means the hemisphere of Qaia that is permanently below Primus — the half of the globe whose surface point is more than the visibility angle away from the sub-Primus point. In everyday terms: stand at the sub-Primus point (0°N, 0°E); all places on the opposite hemisphere are on the far side and cannot see Primus at any time.

Yes: for any observer who is on the near side and within Primus's visibility limits, Primus is motionless in the sky. It literally never rises, never sets, never drifts. That is what geostationary means here: Primus's orbital plane matches Qaia's equator, and its orbital period equals Qaia's sidereal day, so it hangs over the same longitude forever.

Two practical consequences:
- For near-side navigators you can measure elevation whenever you like — there is no timing constraint — and use the primsine lookup plus your pole-star latitude to get longitude without a chronometer.
- For observers near the visibility edge precision drops: Primus sits low on the horizon there and atmospheric refraction and measurement errors make longitude fixes much worse. And on the far side there is no fix at all; they must rely on the alternative methods I mentioned (dead reckoning, stellar transits, or lunar-distance methods using Secundus or Quartus). The NAVIGATION notes also point out this is basically the same difficulty Earth sailors had before chronometers.

Want me to show a simple primsine example (how elevation turns into longitude) or describe the lunar-distance method the notes mention?

Hey — I'm a Qaringil gondolier and a member of the Gondolier guild. Quick question for when I finish my watch: how bright and how big is Primus in Qaia's sky? Is it anything like the big dramatic moons you see in Forgotten Realms or other settings?

Nice — short answer first, then numbers. Primus is small and relatively faint compared to the big, showy moons you might be imagining.

Key facts (from the setting notes, same in Qaringil or Qamis eras):
- Apparent diameter: 10.4 arcminutes — about 0.34 times the angular size of Luna (the reference moon). In plain terms, roughly one-third the apparent diameter of Luna.
- Full-moon brightness: magnitude −9.62. The notes say this is 3.12 magnitudes dimmer than Luna and gives about 0.056× Luna in full-moon illumination (about 1/18 of Luna's full-moon light).
- Surface albedo: 0.06 (dark, iron-rich).
- Unique behavior: Primus is geostationary — it never rises, sets, or drifts for observers on the near side; it sits at a fixed point in the sky over the sub-Primus point.

So compared to the big, dramatic single-moon images you might have from other settings, Primus is a modest, stationary disk — noticeable, but not a dominant source of moonlight.
Okay, numbers are good. But in Forgotten Realms some moons are obvious and light up the world. Would Primus ever look like that, or is it more like a bright star or small coin in the sky?

Primus would come off more like a small, fixed disk — definitely larger and brighter than a typical star, but much smaller and much less luminous than a full Luna or the larger Qaian moons.

Stick to the setting numbers: full Primus gives about 5.6% of Luna's illumination (≈1/18). That is enough to be plainly visible as a little disk to the naked eye and to cast a faint extra glow on a clear night, but it will not light the landscape like Quartus or Tertius can when they are full. The setting even lists sky-size ordering: Tertius (33.7') > Quartus (29.6') > Secundus (28.0') > Primus (10.4'). So Primus is noticeably smaller than the other three main moons.
If I'm standing in Qaringil — will I even see Primus? The city is on that island between the bridges.

The moon system is the same in the Qaringil era, but the campaign notes do not give Qaringil's longitude or its relation to the sub-Primus point, so I can't say for sure whether Qaringil is on the near side that sees Primus.

What I can tell you about visibility from the notes:
- The sub-Primus point is fixed at latitude 0°, longitude 0° (the prime meridian).
- Primus is visible from roughly ±81.3° geocentric angular distance on the equator — in practice: observers within roughly ±81° longitude of the prime meridian on the near hemisphere can see it. That visibility window narrows toward higher latitudes.

If your part of Qaringil lies within that near-side window, you'll see a motionless 10.4' disk in the same spot every night. If Qaringil is on the far side, Primus is permanently below the horizon and you won't see it at all. The notes leave Qaringil's map coordinates blank, so that's one of the map details the DM hasn't fixed yet.
You said there's no phase to wait for — does Primus not have phases? In FR the moon phases can matter for rituals and mood.

The notes say: "Because Primus hangs motionless, there is no special time to observe it, no phase to wait for, and no oscillation to track." That phrasing means two things:
- Primus being geostationary makes it unusually convenient to observe at any time — you don't have to wait for it to rise.
- The notes do not treat Primus's phase cycle as a useful daily timing cue the way the moving moons are.
