Given their small apparent sizes and distances, large total eclipses of the sun or big moons would be rare or very limited; the notes only highlight Quartus and Tertius as more significant for eclipses (Quartus produces a natural monthly cycle and eclipse season due to its inclination).

Navigation and calendar use:
- Navigation: Primus (the geostationary moon) is the navigation anchor for longitude; Sextus/Septimus are not part of the Primus-based method. The notes do not assign a practical navigational role to Sextus; they could be used with star catalogs for celestial fixes, but that is not described in the docs.
- Calendar: Septimus's ~106-day period is explicitly called out as a "useful long-calendar body" in the notes. Sextus (57.69 days) is shorter and could be used for sub-annual markers, but the notes single out Septimus for long-period calendaring. The main calendar drivers mentioned are Tertius (8.15 days → week) and Quartus (29.5 days → month). 

So visually bright but small; retrograde motion; negligible tide influence; Septimus is the one the notes flag as handy for longer-calendar bookkeeping.

What about composition and origin? The notes say Primus and Secundus are iron-rich — are Sextus and Septimus captured asteroids, fragments, or something else? And are they tidally locked or migrating?

What the notes actually say:

Composition
- Primus and Secundus are explicitly high density (6,690 kg/m^3) and are described as near-iron, likely fragments of a differentiated parent body. Tertius is iron-enriched (5,351 kg/m^3). 
- Sextus and Septimus are listed at lunar density (3,344 kg/m^3) — the notes describe these as "physically identical" to each other and as standard rocky (lunar-density) moons. So the setting distinguishes a density split: some moons are iron-rich fragments (Primus, Secundus), some iron-enriched (Tertius), and the rest are lunar-density rock (Sextus, Septimus, Quartus).

Origin
- The notes suggest Primus and Secundus probably come from a differentiated progenitor (hence iron-rich). For Sextus and Septimus, the docs do not give a specific origin story (captured asteroids vs. fragments vs. co-formation). They are simply catalogued as lunar-density bodies; any particular origin beyond that is a blank space the DM has not filled in.

Tidal locking and migration
- The notes state the three freely-orbiting inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) are "almost certainly synchronously rotating" — tidal locking timescale comment is made in that context. The docs do not explicitly state whether Sextus and Septimus are tidally locked. Given that tidal locking timescales scale steeply with orbital distance (a^6), the notes do not commit to a locking state for the more distant small moons.
- Migration: Secundus (retrograde) is noted to be slowly spiraling inward over geological time. The notes do not say whether Sextus or Septimus are migrating inward or outward; no explicit long-term migration behavior for them is given.

Stability
- The 2,000-year simulation in the notes shows all five freely-orbiting moons remaining stable over that interval. The notes also say longer runs might reveal late escapes and that the system's long-term fate without dragon magic is an open question. So stability is supported at least on millennial timescales in the provided sims.

Bottom line: composition-wise Sextus and Septimus are ordinary lunar-density rocky moons (not the iron fragments). Their specific origin story and detailed tidal/migration state are not specified in the notes.
Fine. One last thing: anyone in the setting care about them culturally or use them in myths? Also, are Sextus/Septimus ever mentioned in navigational almanacs or the triple-moon alignment stuff?

Cultural role and myths
- The campaign notes do not attach specific myths, cults, or cultural practices to Sextus or Septimus. The documents call out many "blank spaces" on the map and in lore that the DM intends to fill later; cultural details around the smaller moons are among those not specified. If you want a cultural hook, the notes explicitly allow players/GM to invent local lore for blank spaces.

Almanacs and alignments
- The detailed triple-moon alignment discussion in MOONS.md centers on the three inner, visually significant moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Those three are the drivers for the triple-full/new alignments the notes tabulate (and the ~12-year full/new era cycle). 
- Sextus and Septimus are not part of that triple-moon alignment table; they have much smaller illumination and are not used in the triple-moon alignment calendar discussed in the notes.

Navigation almanacs
- The navigation guide (NAVIGATION.md) uses Primus as the longitude anchor and the pole star for latitude. The notes give a permanent "primsine" table for Primus and mention practical almanac tables for that method. Sextus and Septimus are not used in that Primus-based longitude method, and the notes do not list Sextus/Septimus in standard navigation tables.

So: no explicit cultural or almanac roles given for Sextus/Septimus in the documents. They exist as well-characterized physical bodies, useful as astronomical spectacle and (in Septimus's case) a long-period calendar reference, but detailed human (or Qamian) folklore and formal uses beyond that are left blank by the DM.

Quick question: I'm building a half-elven Edran noble PC and I want them tied to West Artkos. What's West Artkos and why does it matter? Also I'm a bit fuzzy — is West Artkos part of the Qaringil-era map or the present Qamis map? I think Rathakos was there too? Sorry, I might be mixing eras.

Good instincts to check eras — you are mixing things a little, so I will separate them. Tell me if you want only one era.

Short version:
- West Artkos is the historic heartland of Artkos in the present-day Qamis documents (post-Ryraxian War). It matters because Artkos draws political and cultural legitimacy from West Artkos as it attempts to rebuild continental dominance.
- In the Qaringil prequel (about 200 years earlier) Artkos is only its historical heartland in the northwest as well, but the political map is different: the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r in that era and Rathakos/Acrokos do not yet exist.

Now the details that matter to a half-elven Edran noble PC:
Okay, so if my PC is half-elven and from Jia (Edrad), how does West Artkos factor into dynastic politics? Could an Edran noble realistically have claims or rivalries there?

Facts from the notes you should anchor to:
- Edrad is historically the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis; ruled by half-elven nobility from the coastal capital Jia. The Keyberos dynasty has ruled for centuries and was just restored after the Ryraxian War (this is Qamis present-day).
- Artkos (present-day) is governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth and seeks to restore its former dominance. Its historic heart is West Artkos — that history is why Artkos claims authority and why West Artkos matters.

What the notes do not specify (blank spaces): specific noble families with cross-border claims, landed estates of Edran nobles in West Artkos, or precise treaties after the war. Those are not filled in and are fair game to negotiate with your DM or use as PC backstory.

What is plausible and safe to build on: an Edran noble could reasonably have dynastic ties, marriage claims, commercial interests, or old estates in West Artkos because:
- Edrad and Artkos have competed for influence; border and exclave politics are a recurring theme.
- The restored Keyberos dynasty and Artkos’s desire to reclaim dominance make noble marriages, disputes over lands, and claims common sources of tension.

If you want more concrete hooks I can suggest a few that stick to the notes and leave blanks for the DM to confirm.
Yes please — suggest some hooks that use only what's in the notes and clearly flag any invented bits so I can clear them with the DM.

Here are a few hook ideas grounded in the notes, with explicit flags for where the DM must fill in blanks:

1) Marriage alliance strain (low invention)
- Grounded facts: Artkos seeks to restore dominance; Edrad is powerful and ruled by half-elven nobility from Jia.
- Hook: your house arranged a marriage with an Artkos noble from West Artkos generations ago. After the war the alliance has frayed as Artkos tries to reassert itself. You return from Jia to press the claim.
- Blank to confirm with DM: the Artkos noble's family name, whether the treaty still has legal force, and whether West Artkos recognizes your family's claim.

2) Commercial estate in West Artkos (merchant angle)
- Grounded facts: Kard is a financial/shipping center; Artkos has exclaves; Edrad is economically influential from Jia.
- Hook: your family holds land or banking interests that span the border; West Artkos control affects your income and prestige.
- Blank: specific estate location, legal status after the war, and local officials' attitudes.

3) Border grievance and military obligation (noble duty)
- Grounded facts: West Artkos is the historic heart of Artkos; border regions like Rathakos are unstable (Rathakos exists as a separate state in Qamis present-day).
- Hook: your house must decide whether to support Edrad's policies against Artkos expansion or to press a conciliatory line for their West Artkos holdings.
- Blank: exact military arrangements, whether your house has levies stationed there, and local partisan groups.

4) Dynastic legitimacy and Keyberos restoration (court intrigue)
- Grounded facts: Keyberos dynasty was restored in Edrad after the war; Edrad nobility is half-elven and has strict rules about fraction of elf blood.
- Hook: as a half-elven noble you are courted by factions in Jia who want to use your family ties to influence negotiations with Artkos over West Artkos.
- Blank: which court faction specifically supports you, and any specific promises made.

All of these stay within the documented political themes (Edrad vs Artkos, Keyberos restoration, West Artkos as Artkos heartland) and mark where you need DM particulars. Want one turned into a short three-scene intro you can bring to the table?
Yeah, turn the marriage alliance one into a three-scene intro. Keep it strictly to the notes and mark any blank items I should ask the DM about.

Three-scene intro (strictly using setting facts; blanks flagged):

Scene 1 — Jia, a private audience
- Facts used: Jia is the coastal capital of Edrad; Edrad nobility is half-elven and Keyberos dynasty restored.
- Beat: The PC is summoned to a private audience in Jia to discuss an old marriage alliance with an Artkos family tied to West Artkos. Court factions in Jia see the alliance as a lever over Artkos.
- DM blanks to confirm: the Artkos family's name and rank; the exact legal standing of the old promise; any personal history between the PC's house and that family.

Scene 2 — Crossing the border into Artkos holdings
- Facts used: West Artkos is Artkos's historic heartland and matters politically; Artkos under High King Ezevir Arth seeks to restore dominance.
- Beat: The PC travels toward West Artkos to cement or renounce the alliance. The political tone is tense because Artkos is rebuilding influence after the war.
- DM blanks to confirm: the local reception (welcoming, hostile, neutral), whether there are formal checkpoints, and which local officials hold sway in West Artkos.

Scene 3 — The family chamber in West Artkos
- Facts used: Artkos as a polity values West Artkos as its historic heart; the broader context is post-war reconstruction and rival claims.
- Beat: The PC negotiates with their intended (or their intended's kin). The outcome affects the PC's standing in Jia and Artkos's next moves.
- DM blanks to confirm: the intended's identity and motivations, any oath text, and concrete consequences (land transfer, recognition, or repudiation).

These scenes keep to documented political tensions and leave the local specifics as DM blanks you can request. Want the scenes written as short roleplay prompts or NPC dialogue snippets next?

HEY — old grognard here. Cut the sales pitch: whats a "full-moon era" vs a "new-moon era" in this setting? Sounds like homebrew noise. In Forgotten Realms there's one moon — is this just fluff or does it actually change anything?

Short answer: it's a real, physical pattern in Qaia's moons, not just fluff. The notes describe three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) that sometimes all come to full at almost the same time or all come to new at almost the same time. When the system tends to produce frequent simultaneous fulls for those three moons over several years, that's called a "full-moon era." When it tends to produce frequent simultaneous news over several years, that's a "new-moon era." The dominance alternates in multi-year blocks, with a full cycle of roughly 12 years.

Why that matters physically: the three inner moons together drive Qaia's oscillating tidal load. Their tidal contributions summed are about 4.4 times Earth's lunar tide (Secundus ~1.47x, Tertius ~2.04x, Quartus ~0.87x). So when those three line up (new or full), you get especially strong syzygy effects on tides. The moon mechanics and the triple-era concept are from MOONS.md and the shared navigation notes, not player fiat.
Alright, so is "within 5%" meaningful? You gave numbers — what does 5% mean here? And are we talking illumination or orbital phase? Also, any examples so I can compare to real dates?

Good question. In the triple-alignment table the percentages refer to each moon's illumination (percent full) at the listed moment. "Within 5% of full or new" means the three moons are each within 5 percentage points of either 100% illumination (full) or 0% (new). The notes say:
- All three inner moons are within 5% of full or new about five times per year.
- They are within 1% about once per year.
- The best alignments inside an era can be essentially exact — the notes say "all three moons within 0.1% of syzygy simultaneously."

Concrete examples from the provided decade (2253–2263):
- Feb 13, 2253 at 18h PMT is a near-exact triple-full (Secundus 100.0%, Tertius 100.0%, Quartus 99.8%).
- Apr 26, 2256 at 00h PMT is a near-exact triple-new (Secundus 0.1%, Tertius 0.0%, Quartus 0.2%).
- May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT is the closest triple-new of that decade (all three within 0.1%).
Those dates and the whole 2253–2263 table come from the MOONS.md triple-moon alignment calendar.

So compared to FR where you might get one lunar eclipse or mystical full moon events — does this mean epic once-a-decade stuff here? Or are fulls and news common? I'm skeptical: why would cults or magic care?

Physically, simultaneous triple-fulls and triple-news are not once-a-decade curiosities — the system produces alignments several times a year (five times/year within 5%), and about once per year within 1%. What is rarer is the very best alignments (within 0.1%), and the multi-year eras (blocks of years when fulls are common vs blocks when news are common) that alternate on a roughly 12-year cycle.

On whether cults or magic "should" care: the official notes do not state any cultural or supernatural rules tying magic or religion to those eras. The MOONS.md and NAVIGATION.md document the physical astronomy and tidal effects; they do not invent canonical mystical effects. That is explicitly a blank space for the DM to use. So mechanically, the setting provides a believable astronomical hook (stronger combined tides at syzygy, periodic eras of aligned fulls or news) that you or the DM can attach cultural, religious, or magical consequences to — but those consequences are not specified in the notes.

Fair enough. If I wanted to run a campaign where priests or were-creatures behaved differently in a full-moon era, would that contradict the setting? And remind me again which moons are doing this — I don't want to mix eras with the Qaringil prequel stuff.

You would not contradict the setting by making cults, were-creatures, or magic sensitive to the triple-moon eras — the notes leave those cultural and magical responses as blank spaces for the DM. The moons and the alignment behavior are canonical; how societies or magic interpret them is left open.

To keep eras straight: the moon system (Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, Septimus, plus the tiny Quintus at L4) is shared across both campaigns (Qamis present and the Qaringil prequel). The triple-moon alignment concept specifically concerns the three inner freely-orbiting moons: Secundus (retrograde, ~4.53 day period), Tertius (prograde, ~8.15 day period), and Quartus (prograde, ~29.5 day period). Primus is geostationary and not part of the oscillating tidal cycle (it creates a static ~2 cm bulge). The triple-era timing and the 2253–2263 calendar are examples from MOONS.md and are not tied to one campaign-era only — they illustrate the system's behavior.
Last bit: if I want a low-magic grog feel, what's the simplest, most plausible way to use eras without turning it into a werewolf soap opera? Keep it subtle.

Subtle, low-magic hooks that respect the astronomy: keep the effects ecological and social rather than supernatural. A few simple ideas (explicitly optional; not in the canonical notes):
- Tide-dependent commerce: during strong syzygies (the close triple-fulls or triple-news) coastal merchants and pilots expect unusual tidal ranges and schedule cargo or maintenance windows accordingly. That gives the eras practical weight without magic.
- Religious festival scheduling: temples of Bahamut or local cults mark the recurring strong syzygies as auspicious/inauspicious and hold observances; the frequency (several times a year, with especially notable ones once per year) fits routine ritual calendars.
- Fishing & harbors: certain fisheries or tidal flats yield differently around the big alignments, so coastal villages develop lore and minor taboos tied to full-era vs new-era expectations.

All of those lean on the documented tidal physics (combined tidal load ~4.4x Luna when the three act together) and on the documented cadence of alignments, without introducing mandatory supernatural changes to PCs or monsters. The notes explicitly leave cultural/magical consequences open, so treating them as local custom or ecological pressure fits the setting and keeps the tone old-school and plausible.

Hey — I missed last session and I'm thinking about running the Qaringil prequel as a one-shot. Quick question first: how much of Westron is actually defined in the setting notes? I need to know whether I have to invent most of it or if there are canon bits I should fit to.

Short answer: almost none of it is defined. Both the Qaris/Qamis notes treat Westron as a large, important-but-undeveloped continent. Exact wording in the notes (paraphrased):

- In the Qaringil (prequel) notes: "The continent to the West is important and I have not yet prepared any material! There are religious differences, with them mostly not being Bahumet worshipers."
- In the Qamis (present) notes: "Maybe it should have a better name. It's the continent to the west. It's exotic or some shit. It is very large."

So: Westron is explicitly a blank space the DM has not prepared. That means you are free to invent geography, nations, cultures, religions, and factions there — but you should mark it as a DM-created addition rather than canonical detail from the notes.

Because you're looking at the Qaringil prequel specifically: the notes for that era also emphasize what is defined around Qaringil itself (Holy Qis Empire ruling from Ath'r, the island city-state of Qaringil with its guilds and Five Families, the wizard-made isthmus and the Great Western Bridge). Westron remains undefined in that era as well and is simply noted as "important but unprepared." 

Do you want a very short checklist of the few things you should decide if you want to run a Westron-flavored one-shot (so you can improvise the rest), or a few ready-to-drop one-shot seeds that lean on the "undefined but exotic" framing?

Yes, please — give me a compact checklist of the minimum decisions to make for a one-shot set in or involving Westron, and then three plug-and-play one-shot seeds that don't require me to invent a whole continent.

Checklist (minimum decisions to make for a tight one-shot):

1) Role of Westron in your story: decide whether PCs travel there, meet Westron visitors in Qaringil, or have Westron forces arrive suddenly. Keeping contact limited simplifies prep.
2) Scale: single city/port, a small coastal region, or an expedition into Westron interior. For a one-shot, pick a single port or caravan route.
3) Cultural hook: pick one salient cultural/religious trait (the notes only say "not Bahamut worshipers") — e.g., a ritual, taboo, or priestly order. Keep it narrow and visually distinct so players remember it.
4) Political goal/conflict: a clear, immediate objective (deliver an envoy, stop a kidnapping, escort a merchant, retrieve a relic). No multi-front geopolitics needed.
5) Key NPCs: 2–3 named faces (envoy/captain/local priest/merchant) with one clear motivation each. Use existing Qaringil factions (guilds or Five Families) to justify contact if you want.
6) Stakes and timer: a single ticking element (ship leaves at dawn, festival ends at midnight, a bridge toll collector arrives) to keep the one-shot focused.
7) Local color/props: 3 sensory details (food, dress, ritual chant, a conspicuous sigil) so the setting feels real without much lore.

Three plug-and-play one-shot seeds that avoid inventing a full nation:

Seed A — The Envoy's Riddle
- Premise: A Westron envoy arrives at Qaringil's Great Western Bridge bearing a sealed message and strange non-Bahamut ritual beads. The envoy collapses before delivering it.
- Objective: Protect the envoy, discover what the message was, decide whether to unseal it or hand it to the Exarch or a Five Family.
- Complications: A rival guild wants the envoy's cargo; the envoy's ritual beads react badly to Quartus's phase (or some celestial omen you choose); the envoy's body is being targeted by a small cult who believes the beads are sacred.
- Why it fits Qaringil prequel: keeps action in the city, uses Great Western Bridge and guild politics, introduces Westron as exotic without needing more.

Seed B — Smuggler's Anchor
- Premise: A wrecked Westron longboat washes up near Qaringil with a surviving passenger clutching a forbidden idol and speaking an unfamiliar tongue.
- Objective: Escort the survivor to guild custody, figure out who wants the idol, and decide whether to hand it over, keep it, or return it to Westron hands (if possible).
- Complications: Thieves' and Bandits' guilds both want the idol; the Wool guild fears trade sanctions; the survivor's religion provokes street riots.
- Why it fits: uses existing guild rivalry and the city's criminal "guilds" without committing to Westron-wide details.

Seed C — Festival of Strange Fire
- Premise: During a Qaringil festival, a visiting troupe of Westron performers demonstrates a ritual fire-dance tied to their religion. The ritual accidentally summons a planar trickster or minor elemental.
- Objective: Contain the summoned creature, prevent panic, and learn whether the ritual was a deliberate provocation or a tragic mistake.
- Complications: Banking and Shipping guilds pressure you to keep the scandal quiet; a Five Family wants to use the incident for political leverage; the performers claim asylum.
- Why it fits: provides a clear mechanical challenge (contain/banish) and social choices, all within the city.

If you want, I can give you one fully-sketched NPC (motivation, appearance, one secret) and a hook you can drop straight into the session for each seed. Which seed do you prefer, or do you want the NPCs for all three?

Great — give me one NPC for Seed A (Envoy's Riddle) that I can drop in: appearance, motivation, one secret, and a quick stat-block suggestion or role in skill challenges (no need for full 5e stats). Also, any quick note about how the "not Bahamut" religious difference might play visually or mechanically in the scene.

NPC for Seed A — Envoy's Riddle

Name and role (you can rename if you want): the Westron Envoy, a tall, gaunt human-looking diplomat who claims to represent a coastal port of Westron. He is carried on a litter into the bridge guildhall, breathing shallowly.

Appearance: weathered, dark-bronze skin, hair plaited with short bone beads, a simple heavy cloak dyed in a single, deep color unfamiliar to Qaringil locals. He wears a string of matte stone beads across his chest (the "ritual beads") and carries a sealed brass scroll tube hanging from his belt.

Motivation: deliver the sealed message to a particular Qaringil authority and secure asylum for a friend back home. He fears that a rival political faction in Westron will seize his coastal city.

One secret (revealable): the beads are a ferrying talisman used in a Westron death-rite — they slow a spirit tied to the envoy's person. If opened or damaged, the spirit's hold weakens and it may lash out. The envoy knows this only partially; he believes the beads will protect him until the message is delivered.

Role in skill challenges (quick, non-stat guidance):
- Stabilize/Medicine: someone must tend the envoy (DC moderate) or he dies, taking the sealed message into uncertain hands.
- Investigation/Insight: reading the envoy's manner and bead arrangement gives a clue that the beads are ritual (tool proficiency in Religious or Arcana gives advantage/hints).
- Persuasion (or Performance): convincing the envoy to reveal where to deliver the tube (Exarch, a Family, guildmaster) or calming the crowd.
- Stealth/Deception: a rival guild may try to steal the tube; a successful sleight/steal contest or distraction is needed to intercept.

How to run the reveal: if the players or rival agents try to force open or damage the beads, have them witness a small spectral effect (a whispering shadow, a chill that unnerves) and trigger a short combat/containment scene involving a weak spirit that the beads were suppressing. That spirit is dangerous mostly as a panic trigger and a social complication — it's not a major monster unless you want it to be.

Quick note on the "not Bahamut" religious difference (visual/mechanical cues):
- Visual shorthand: Westron religious practice should look and feel distinct from Bahamut rites — different symbols, music, and taboos. Use a single striking visual (e.g., beads, a braided cord, or a hand gesture) rather than building an entire pantheon.
- Mechanical shorthand: the PC interaction can hinge on misunderstanding. For example, a Qaringil temple official may react with confusion or suspicion rather than automatic devotion; street crowds may misinterpret rituals as blasphemy and riot. If you want a mechanical hook, treat the beads/ritual as a local "religious trigger" that imposes social penalties (disadvantage on Persuasion with religious NPCs) until someone successfully explains or bridges the cultural gap (Religion or Persuasion check).

Want a one-paragraph scene opener you can read to the table to drop this in?
yooo when's the next great triple-new alignment?

Nice question — do you mean in the present Qamis campaign or the Qaringil prequel? The moons data that has the alignment calendar is shared by both campaigns, so I can answer using that table.

Most concisely: the closest "great" triple-new of the decade in the moons reference is 2262 May 24 at 03h PMT — the table calls that the closest triple-new of the 2253–2263 decade (all three inner moons within 0.1%). Other very-close triple-new dates in that decade are 2256 Apr 26 and 2256 Sep 21 (both listed at 0.0–0.2%, bold in the table). 

If by "next" you mean relative to some in-game date (like your campaign year or tonight in Rathakos), tell me what in-world date your table uses or which year the DM has the campaign in and I can point to the next one. Also, do you want the list of all triple-new dates in the decade?
oh cool. quick thing — what's PMT? and how would I know if it's happening tonight in-game?

The calendar uses the label "PMT" for the timestamp, but the campaign notes include those datestamps without defining PMT anywhere I can find. So the notes do not say exactly what PMT stands for or how it maps to local time — you should ask the DM which time standard they mean (likely a campaign-wide planetary mean time, but that is not stated in the docs).

About spotting a triple-new in play: the table shows the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) with percent illumination near 0% on triple-new dates, so observationally those moons will be at or very near new phase — i.e., they will not be giving significant night illumination. If your DM is using the table, they may announce a noticeably darker night or say the three moons are absent/near the sun. To convert a PMT timestamp into "local" time you'd need the DM's definition of PMT and your party's longitude or the campaign's local time rules; the notes do say the sub-Primus point defines longitude 0° (the prime meridian), but they do not provide a PMT->local conversion rule.

If you tell me (a) what campaign year the DM is running or (b) your PCs' location/longitude and what the DM says PMT means, I can help work out whether May 24, 2262 03h PMT would fall on your next in-game night.
hello — quick question: how much do we actually know about Westron? I thought Freeport and the dragons were from there, and that it's full of non-Bahamut stuff. Am I mixing things up?

Good question, and welcome — it's great you're checking this before the game. A quick correction first, since it's easy to conflate things between the two eras in the notes.

- Freeport and the "big island to the south" are distinct from Westron. Freeport sits on that big southern island (the notes say "Some say this is where the dragons went"). That southern island is not Westron.
- Westron is specifically "the continent to the west." It appears in both the Qamis (present) and Qaringil (prequel) notes, but the DM has not filled in much detail for it.

What the notes actually say about Westron:
- Qamis (present): "Maybe it should have a better name. It’s the continent to the west. It’s exotic or some shit, I guess. It is very large." (i.e., mostly undefined)
- Qaringil (prequel): "The continent to the West is important and I have not yet prepared any material! There are religious differences, with them mostly not being Bahumet worshipers." So the only extra hint in the prequel notes is that Westron people tend not to worship Bahamut.

So: you were right that there are "non-Bahamut" religious differences mentioned, but you mixed up Freeport/the southern island with Westron. Beyond that, Westron is deliberately a blank space the DM can expand or let players fill in.
Okay — I'm new to D&D and not sure what to do with that. Can I make my character from Westron? What should I ask the DM if I want a Westron background?

Yes — Westron is an excellent choice for a player-origin because it gives you room to define culture, religion, trade ties, and rivalries. Because the notes leave it open, anything you invent should be checked with the DM, but it's a great collaborative opportunity. Useful things to ask the DM:

- Which era are we playing in (Qamis present starting in Rathakos, or the Qaringil prequel)? That changes political context.
- Do you want Westron to be a major power or a loosely sketched exotic continent in this campaign? (DM may prefer one or the other.)
- Any high-level tone preferences: frontier/backwater, mercantile, theocratic, colonial, fractured city-states, etc.?
- Language: will Westron have its own dialect/language or use one of the existing prestige languages listed in the notes? (Remember: there are no racial languages; main national languages are Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas.)
- Religion: the prequel notes hint Westron is "mostly not Bahumet worshipers". Does the DM want a distinct dominant faith, or a mix?
- Any major cities, ports, or notable organizations (merchants, pirate havens) the DM already has in mind or wants players to invent?

If you want, I can draft a short Westron-origin outline you can show the DM — but I won't invent specific place names or politics beyond what's in the notes unless the DM OKs it.
That sounds good. Could you give me three one-paragraph origin blurb options to bring to the DM? Keep them generic so they don't contradict notes.

Sure — here are three short, intentionally generic origin blurbs you can show the DM. None of these invent named cities or political facts beyond the notes.

1) "I grew up on the western continent of Westron, a large and exotic land my tutors only hinted about. My family were traders who crossed the sea bringing spices and strange fabrics; I learned to read a merchant ledger and listen to coarse sailors. When a storm sank our ship and left us ashore here, I kept the bookkeeping skills and a few strange trinkets from home as proof I belonged to a place the locals consider foreign."

2) "My childhood was spent in a mountain hamlet on Westron, far from the worship halls of Bahamut. Our village followed a local faith and old weather rites; I trained as an artisan and sometimes led small ritual dances to keep storms away. Seeking luck and work, I took passage east to the central sea and landed near Rathakos. I bear Westron customs and songs that mark me as an outsider."

3) "Born into a Westron shipwright family, I learned to read lines and splice ropes. Westron society where I came from is diverse and not dominated by Bahamut clerics—our guilds answer to local councils. I traveled east for hire as a deckhand and got caught up in the war’s refugee flow. I still keep a small tool token from my apprenticeship, and I speak with an accent the locals notice."

Show any of these to the DM and ask which fits. The DM can anchor details (trade goods, religious rites, region names) if they want a consistent setting, or leave it loose for you to roleplay.
Awesome, thanks. One last thing: you mentioned the two eras — which one should I be assuming for a first session starting in Rathakos?

For a session starting in Rathakos, assume the Qamis present-day campaign. The Qamis campaign notes explicitly say the campaign will start in Rathakos and that the setting is post-Ryraxian War (Ryraxos exiled to Secundus, Keyberos restored in Edrad). The Qaringil prequel is a separate campaign set about 200 years earlier and is based on the island city-state of Qaringil.

So if your game session is the main Qamis campaign, use the Qamis-era context (post-war politics, Rathakos as a border principality, etc.). If the DM meant the Qaringil prequel instead, they will say so, but Rathakos as a starting city belongs to the Qamis notes.
Perfect. Anything else I should know as a total newbie about using blank regions like Westron in my character backstory?

A few quick tips:

- Treat blank regions as collaborative space: propose details but ask the DM to confirm or adapt them so they fit the campaign's tone.
- Keep one or two tangible hooks (a family heirloom, an accent, a guild or deity preference) you and the DM can use in scenes; avoid long, intricate histories unless the DM wants them.
- Use existing setting anchors when helpful: mention trade with the central sea, or a contrast to Bahamut-dominated Qis’mos, but avoid inventing major nations or events that the notes do not list.
- If you want the DM to develop Westron later, offer to write a short paragraph or map seed; DMs often appreciate player input on blank spaces.

If you want, tell me the class and concept you plan and I can tailor one of the blurbs to lean more toward combat, magic, or social hooks before you show it to the DM.
Hey — quick intro: my PC is a drow who stayed on the surface after the war. What world does this campaign take place on? Give me the big picture and anything that affects a surface-drow.

Short version: the world is the planet Qaia, the continent is Qamis, and the present-day campaign era is called Qamis (post-Ryraxian War). Important high-level facts from the notes:

- The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and just ended. The dragon Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon (Secundus) and will play no further role in the campaign. The Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad and Artkos’s High King and other rulers are trying to reassert control.
- The campaign starts in Rathakos (a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains). That is explicitly where the DM expects play to begin.
- Drow are present on the surface: they were recruited as allies by Artkos during the war and remain in a dozen scattered enclaves across the surface. The most prominent drow-held place is the holy city Qama (seat of the Church of Bahamut) in Qis’mos, which the drow occupied in the late war.
- Qama was not despoiled by the drow; they appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, producing a schism with an alternate candidate (Qachu II) operating from Qis but lacking the relics of office.

What this means for a surface-drow PC: there is canonical precedent for drow living openly on the surface and even holding institutional power (at least in Qama). Beyond that, many social and legal details are left blank in the notes (see below).
Okay — so are drow openly accepted elsewhere on the surface? Do they have citizenship, rights, or are they pariahs? I want to know what my character might face socially and legally.

The notes do not specify general social or legal status for drow outside the few facts above. What is stated:

- Drow were recruited by Artkos as allies during the war and now remain in scattered enclaves on the surface (about a dozen). Qama is the single prominent example.
- In Qama the drow have enough control to appoint a high priest (Qe'lth IV), which implies significant local influence. The city was not despoiled by them.

What the notes are silent on (the DM has left these as blank spaces):

- Whether drow have formal citizenship in Edrad, Artkos, Qis’mos, Rathakos, etc.
- Whether surface populations generally accept or discriminate against drow, or whether their status varies regionally.
- Specific laws, travel restrictions, guild access, or official treatment of drow in places besides Qama.

If you need concrete answers at the table, those are things to adjudicate with your DM or to treat as character hooks: regional variation (friendly enclave vs. hostile border town), grudging accommodation in places where drow were wartime allies, or active suspicion in areas that fought on the other side.
Got it. What about religion — Bahamut and Qama? Can a drow be a cleric of Bahamut? Is the church tolerant?

From the notes:

- The Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence in Qis’mos and its traditional seat is the holy city Qama.
- Drow occupied Qama in the late phases of the war and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, causing a schism; an alternate candidate (Qachu II) operates from Qis but lacks the relics of office.

The notes do not say whether the broader Church hierarchy accepts drow clerics, nor do they give rules about ordination, lay tolerance, or how worship is practiced in drow-held Qama. The schism implies political and doctrinal friction, but specifics (are drow clerics recognized elsewhere, are there bans, are there congregations of mixed race) are blank.

If you want your character to be a Bahamut cleric, that fits the established facts (drow are in control in Qama and the church there is active). The DM will need to decide how other clergy and regions treat that ordination and whether the schism affects your character's standing.
I care about nights — my PC prefers darkness. What do the moons look like? Any special moon facts that affect a drow who avoids daylight?

Moon system facts from MOONS.md (shared across both campaigns):

- There are five main moons plus a trace particle (Quintus). Key visible moons:
  - Quartus: basically a Luna twin (angular diameter ~29.6', full brightness -12.64). The natural monthly body; period ~29.5 days.
  - Tertius: slightly larger in the sky than Quartus (mean 33.7') and nearly as bright (full -12.60). Period ~8.15 days (basis of the ~8-day week).
  - Secundus: retrograde, nearly lunar in size (mean 28.0') but darker (full -11.76); period ~4.53 days. Secundus is the moon Ryraxos was exiled to (so lore tie-in).
  - Primus: a tiny, dark, geostationary moon fixed over one hemisphere; apparent diameter ~10.4' and full magnitude -9.62. It never rises or sets for the hemisphere that sees it.
  - Sextus and Septimus: small, faint outer moons (very minor light sources).

Practical points for a surface-drow:

- Nights can be brighter or dimmer depending on which moons are up. Tertius and Quartus can be as bright as Earth's full moon; Secundus is nearly as large but ~1 magnitude dimmer. Combined tidal and illumination cycles are complex: the three inner moons produce an oscillating tidal load about 4.4 times what Luna alone produces.
- Primus is unique: if you are on the hemisphere that sees Primus, it is a fixed, motionless point in the sky (no phases, no rising/setting). If you are on the far hemisphere you never see it.

The notes do not fix which hemisphere Qama or any specific drow enclave lies on, so whether Primus is visible to your PC is not stated. If being hidden from Primus or using Primus as a landmark matters to play, that's another DM/PC decision.
So does the setting name Qamis refer to the continent or the whole world? And what's Qaringil again — is that a different world?

Clarification from the notes:

- Qaia is the planet.
- Qamis is the present-day campaign: the continent on Qaia where the post-war events occur. "Qamis" is the era/setting name for the present-day political map (post-Ryraxian War).
- Qaringil is a different campaign set roughly 200 years earlier on the same world (Qaia). Qaringil is the prequel era: politically the map is different and the campaign is based on the island city-state of Qaringil. The moon system and planetary facts (MOONS.md and NAVIGATION.md) are shared across both eras because they are physical.

So same planet (Qaia), different era/political maps: Qamis = present continental campaign, Qaringil = prequel centered on the island city-state.
I like worldbuilding — what big blank spaces could I lean into for my drow's backstory? Be explicit about what's unspecified in the notes.

Good idea. Explicit blank spaces you can exploit (these are either flagged as blanks in the notes or simply not specified):

- Exact locations, sizes, and cultures of most of the dozen drow enclaves beyond Qama. Latitude/longitude/hemisphere for each enclave is unspecified.
- Legal status and citizenship of drow in the different nations (Edrad, Artkos, Qis’mos, Rathakos, Acrokos, Lock, etc.).
- Social attitudes toward drow in most regions — whether they face systemic discrimination, are tolerated due to wartime alliances, or are persecuted.
- Internal drow politics on the surface: leadership, clans, whether they maintain underground ties, priesthood organization beyond Qe'lth IV.
- Day-to-day commerce, guild participation, and economic niches occupied by surface drow outside Qama.
- Any treaties or formal agreements concerning drow enclaves after the war.
- The far-side hemisphere map and which major cities/regions lie on the Primus-invisible hemisphere. The map images exist but many inland regions are intentionally blank.
- Much of the big island to the south beyond Freeport and the jungle — largely unexplored and flagged as a blank.

Those blanks are intentional hooks. You can define where your character's enclave is, how they were treated after the war, whether they belong to an organized drow polity or a small family group, and how the Qama schism affects your loyalties. Run important choices by the DM, but these are all legitimate places to anchor a deep backstory.
Last thing — I keep seeing "Q is pronounced Kh." Am I pronouncing my name wrong? And is "Qama" pronounced like Khama?

Yes — in-setting note: Q is pronounced "Kh" (this comes from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So pronounce Qama as "Khama." Whenever you see a place or name with Q, say it with a "Kh" sound. That applies across both eras and all place names in the notes (Qamis, Qis, Qaringil, Qe'lth, Qachu, etc.).
hi — quick one. why is Rathakos famous for adventuring? I'm a tech lead who plays D&D on weekends and I want an executive-summary of the world and why the campaign starts there.

Short executive summary (Qamis era, present day): Rathakos is a mountainous border princedom carved out of the Red Mountains after the Ryraxian War. It sits on dangerous, poorly explored terrain and has a strong local tradition of frontiersmanship and adventuring. Politically unstable (no prince, governed by Regent John Dunne and a Grand Council) and bordered by multiple nations, it produces a steady supply of veterans, mercenaries, treasure hunters, and opportunities for work and conflict—exactly the conditions that make it a natural campaign start.

Would you like a concise bullet list of the concrete factors that create that adventuring culture, or a deeper dive into the political and historical background?
Bullet list please — concrete, engineer-friendly reasons (think: inputs -> outputs). Keep it short but specific.

Inputs -> Outputs (Rathakos, Qamis present):

- Input: Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored. Output: ruins, hidden passes, mineral and artifact prospects, and lots of unknown threats to investigate.
- Input: Border location (touches Edrad, Qis’mos, Artkos, Qirkos, Lock, Acrokos). Output: constant cross-border incidents, bounty work, scouts, smugglers, and diplomatic friction creating adventure hooks.
- Input: Recent political upheaval (Red Mountain Republic history; now a Princedom with no prince; Regent John Dunne + Grand Council). Output: power vacuums, rival factions, contested authority — great for intrigue and militia campaigns.
- Input: War aftermath (fought on both sides during Ryraxian War). Output: veterans, displaced people, abandoned fortifications and caches, and unresolved grievances that fuel quests and mercenary work.
- Input: Mountain communities with old dragon cults. Output: cult relics, secret rites, possible ruins tied to draconic worship — both treasure and threat.
- Input: Lots of blank spaces on the map (DM has not filled details). Output: flexible hooks and local lore you can customize or tie to player backstories.

Want me to turn any of these into a 1-2 session adventure seed?
Yes — give me three tight 1-2 session seeds that I could drop into a weekend game. Keep them clearly marked as DM hooks (notes say some map bits are blank so be explicit about where I'm inventing).

Three compact 1–2 session seeds (all set in Rathakos; flagged where the notes are blank):

1) Missing Mine Caravan (low-to-mid threat)
- Premise: A small ore caravan fails to arrive at Rath's market after traversing a narrow mountain pass. Merchants hire the party to find it. 
- Why it fits the setting: Red Mountains + frontier trade routes. Opportunities for combat, tracking, negotiation with smugglers or local clans.
- Blank-space note: the exact pass, NPC merchant names, and the caravan manifest are not specified in the notes — invent those to fit your table.

2) Dragon-Cult Shrine Unearthed (mystery + skirmish)
- Premise: A shepherd/steward uncovers a sealed crag shrine with draconic iconography. Local clerics fear a dragon-cult remnant; the Grand Council sends for investigators. The shrine contains defensive wards, a cultist remnant, and an old draconic relic whose purpose is ambiguous.
- Why it fits: mountain communities that follow old dragon cults + ruins left over from turbulent times.
- Blank-space note: the shrine's exact history, the relic's effects, and cult leadership are not in the notes — make these plot-critical details yourself.

3) Claimant at the Gate (political/skill challenge)
- Premise: A charismatic claimant shows up at Rath claiming lineage or backing to be the missing Prince. He brings armed supporters and asks to be escorted to a rural assembly or to broadcast proof. The party can be hired by the Regent, by rival nobles, or by the claimant himself — social encounters, possible ambushes, and a short battlefield if things go sideways.
- Why it fits: no prince, Regent John Dunne + Grand Council; internal unrest in the Southern Reaches and mountain provinces.
- Blank-space note: the claimant's background and which factions back him are not set in the notes — you can invent motives and attachments.

If you want, I can expand any seed into an encounter-by-encounter outline with NPC stats, likely loot, and complication options.
Nice. One more: how does the Ryraxian War and Ryraxos specifically change play in Rathakos? Keep it factual to the notes.

Facts from the notes (Qamis present):

- The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and has just ended. That conflict reshaped borders and politics across the continent.
- Ryraxos, the bronze dragon who had waged aggressive war, was exiled to the second moon (Secundus) and plays no role in the campaign.
- Rathakos's origin ties to the war: during the conflict the former mountain provinces were organized into the Red Mountain Republic under Edran occupation. When Edran forces withdrew, an opposition faction purged the pro-Edran elements and established the new nations of Rathakos and Acrokos.
- Consequences for Rathakos in play: a legacy of occupation and guerrilla resistance, purges and factionalism, contested loyalties (they fought on both sides at times), and abundant ruins, veterans, and chaos to exploit for plots.

Notes to avoid: the drow presence is explicitly noted in Qama (Qis’mos) after the war, not in Rathakos. Also, Ryraxos is exiled and therefore absent from current events.

Anything else you want tied back to gameplay (e.g., veteran NPC stats, likely factions, or how to seed region-specific treasure)?

hi, who rules Artkos and what do they want? i'm a player thinking about political hooks and mechanical implications for mid-level play — want to know how to model their goals and pressure on neighboring nations.
Quick era check: do you mean Artkos in the present-day Qamis campaign (post-Ryraxian War) or the Qaringil prequel (~200 years earlier)? The notes give rulers/goals for both eras but they differ.
Present-day Qamis — what's the situation there?

Present-day Qamis (post-Ryraxian War):
- Ruler: Artkos is governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth (the notes call him High King Ezevir Arth).
- Stated political aim: Artkos "yearns to restore its dominance over the rest of the Artklan League." The high kings generally see the new Artklan League as a pale shadow of the old empire and want to reassert control.
- Geography/power levers that matter: Artkos holds West Artkos (historic heart) and several exclaves, including the central one containing Ath'r (a long-time seat of central Qamisi empires) and a southwestern enclave centered on the city of Kard (a major financial and shipping center). The notes also say governance in Kard is fragile and much of Kard's population is Edran-speaking and welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago.

What the notes do not specify (blank spaces): precise internal factional balances in Artkos, the size/composition of their army or navy, whether High King Ezevir Arth is personally hawkish or cautious, or details on succession and prominent noble houses. Those are left open by the DM.

Okay — mechanically, how do I translate "yearns to restore dominance" into game terms for a 5th-level party? What sorts of encounters, factions, and escalation mechanics would be appropriate without inventing new named lore?

Good question. Use the setting facts as a political vector and keep specifics (names, sizes) loose or represented as abstract mechanics. Suggestions you can drop straight into a table/campaign: 

1) Faction pressure track
- Model Artkos expansionism as an "influence/pressure" track against neighboring polities (Rathakos, Kard, Edrad-held borderlands). Track tiers like: diplomatic pressure → covert operations/mercenary use → border raids → limited war. Each tier raises stakes and changes encounter types.

2) Typical encounter types by tier (appropriate for mid-level parties)
- Diplomatic pressure: social encounters, espionage missions, skill challenges (Persuasion/Deception/Insight/Investigation) to protect allies or expose forgeries.
- Covert ops/mercenaries: stealth/infiltration adventures, rescuing/convincing local leaders, fighting small groups of trained soldiers or mercenaries (use Veteran or Bandit Captain stat blocks for hardened soldiers; use commoner/guard stat blocks for militia).
- Border raids: patrol combats, ambushes, defended caravans. Patrols = 3–6 Veterans or a mix of Guards + 1 Veteran/captain; caravan defense gives value-based objectives rather than pure HP sink.
- Limited war: sieges, large-scale skirmishes, sabotage missions. For a single 5th-level party, these are best handled as story beats plus a few high-stakes encounters (elite officer, magical support, or a defended objective) rather than simulating thousands of troops.

3) Faction agents and NPC types (no new lore names)
- Artkos diplomat/agent (social challenge): use NPC statblocks for a noble or spy; success/failure shapes local politics.
- Artkos veteran troop (combat): Veteran stat block or a few Guards upgraded with better tactics.
- Artkos officer/captain (boss fight): Veteran or Bandit Captain with leadership tactics.
- Mercenary captain or corsair (naval/maritime pressure): use pirate/sea-captain variants and ship-combat rules if you do naval encounters.

4) Non-combat mechanical levers
- Economic pressure: embargoes that raise costs or deny access to market resources — model this as downtime penalties, higher craft/shop prices, or a gold drain for the city.
- Influence checks: bribery, guild sway, or a "favor" economy — treat as skill checks with escalating DCs as Artkos influence grows.

5) Adventure hooks tied to known geography
- Exploit Kard's fragility: missions to secure or expose loyalty among merchants, disrupt pro-Artkos agents, or win a guild's support.
- Border friction with Rathakos: defend a mountain pass, investigate a burned village, stop a false-flag operation.

If you want, I can convert any of these into explicit 1-3 encounter “mini-sessions” tuned to a 5th-level party (with recommended CRs and encounter compositions). Tell me which hook you want statted.

Yes — stat me up a plausible "Artkos border patrol ambush" and a "Kard covert sabotage" for a party of four 5th-level PCs. Don't invent setting names beyond what's in the notes. Also list what the notes do not tell us that I'd need if I want to escalate to full war.

Ambush (border patrol) — concept and encounter composition
- Scene: a narrow Red Mountains road near Rathakos. Party defends a supply wagon or escorts a scout.
- Threats (use generic D&D statblocks):
  - 3 Veterans (trained Artkos soldiers) — mid-tier combatants who use coordinated tactics.
  - 2 Scouts/Guards (light troops or hunters) — skirmishers using hit-and-run, covering the Veterans.
  - 1 Veteran Sergeant (Veteran or Bandit Captain variant) — leads the patrol and uses tactical Command actions (moves to protect wounded, uses ranged support).

Tactical notes for balance with four 5th-level PCs:
- Veterans (standard Veteran stat block) are CR-appropriate single opponents; a group of 3 + leader + skirmishers should be a challenging but fair fight if terrain favors the defenders. Use rough cover, elevation, and limited sight lines to force the party into meaningful choices: protect the wagon or pursue flanking scouts.
- If the party is likely to overwhelm this easily, replace one Veteran with a stronger leader (Veteran with a multiattack or spellcasting ally) or add a mercenary arbalist (ranged threat).

Kard covert sabotage — concept and main mechanical beats
- Scene: the party must infiltrate a merchant warehouse in Kard to destroy pro-Artkos manifest books or plant evidence linking an Artkos agent to treason.
- Primary challenges: stealth/social skill challenge, a guard rotation, a locked archive, a potential alarm.

Suggested mechanical layout:
- Skill challenge rounds (Stealth/Thieves' Tools/Investigation/Perception/Deception): 3–5 successes before 3 failures. Each PC contributes; failure triggers an alarm (guards alerted).
- Guards on site: 2 Guards (watchmen) + 1 Veteran on duty (captain) if alarm triggers.
- Optional boss: a low-ranking Artkos agent playing the role of investigator (use Spy or Noble statblock). If confronted, they escalate to calling city watch (time pressure).

Rewards and consequences
- Success: deny Artkos intelligence/forge documents, win merchant favor, reduce Artkos influence track by one notch.
- Failure: alarms, arrests, forced escape. Politically, failure can be used by Artkos as "evidence" of unrest to justify increased patrols or punitive measures.

What the notes do NOT provide (critical blanks if you want to model full escalation to open war)
- Precise military strength of Artkos: army size, garrison locations, composition of forces, quality of troops vs. militia. (You can treat Artkos troops as better-trained than local militias, but the notes leave the scale unspecified.)
- Naval strength and doctrine: how Artkos projects power by sea, whether they rely on a royal navy or mercenary fleets from the islands.
- Political factions inside Artkos: hawks vs. moderates, powerful noble houses, or any succession crises.
- Exact relations and treaties with neighboring nations (timelines, defense pacts).
- Whether High King Ezevir Arth personally favors war or prefers diplomatic restoration — his temperament and court politics are blank.
- Logistics and economic capacity for sustained war (tax base, supply lines, key resources).

If you want to escalate to full war mechanics, you'll need to pick values for at least the above blanks. I can offer templates (small war, limited expedition, or total war) with suggested resource pools and timelines if you tell me which direction you'd like to take.

Good. One last thing: how could the PCs realistically slow down Artkos influence without starting full war? Specific mechanical actions I can run at table (skill checks, downtime, factions), prioritized.

Practical, low-escalation actions the PCs can take, prioritized and mapped to game mechanics:

1) Win merchant/guild support in Kard (high impact, low violence)
- Mechanics: Persuasion/Insight/Deception/History checks vs. firm DCs determined by NPC importance; spend downtime/resources to buy favors.
- Effect: reduces Artkos influence in Kard by one notch; merchants resist embargoes and fund local defenses. Failure raises suspicion and may invite covert sabotage.

2) Expose Artkos covert operations (medium impact)
- Mechanics: Investigation/Perception/Stealth to collect evidence, then Diplomacy/Intimidation to present evidence to a local authority or guild. Use a social skill challenge (3 successes before 3 failures).
- Effect: forces Artkos to back off covert ops or reshuffle agents; reduces pressure track escalation.

3) Undermine supply lines (medium impact, some risk)
- Mechanics: Ambush encounters (combat) or sabotage skill checks (Thieves' Tools, Survival for mountain tracks). Time-limited missions increase risk of detection.
- Effect: slows military action, buys time for diplomacy. Repeated strikes risk retaliation if Artkos attributes them to Rathakos/others.

4) Secure political allies (long-term, strategic)
- Mechanics: Diplomacy/Performance/bribery, influence economy (spend gold/downtime to gain bonds), guild favors. Use a ‘‘favor’’ currency: 3–5 favors needed to change a local policy.
- Effect: creates a network of states that can resist Artkos diplomatic pressure; makes escalation politically costly for Artkos.

5) Public relations and propaganda (low risk)
- Mechanics: gather incriminating documents or stage public events; use Performance/Deception/Persuasion to win public sentiment. Could be modeled as contesting an Influence score via skill challenges over multiple sessions.
- Effect: raises the political cost for Artkos to intervene overtly.

6) Targeted diplomacy with moderate Artkos agents (low to medium risk)
- Mechanics: social encounters to find and sway moderates, negotiated pacts, blackmail options. Successful contacts can chill hawkish options.

Practical table implementations
- Use an influence/pressure track with 3–5 steps from "stable" → "strained" → "covert ops" → "border incidents" → "open war." PCs' actions move the track one step per major success/failure.
- Reward players mechanically for non-combat solutions: XP, favors, trade privileges, or mechanical boons (discounts, safehouses, information nodes).
- Make each action have visible short-term and long-term consequences so players can see the political chessboard.

If you want, I can lay out a simple influence-track board and assign DCs, downtime costs, and exact skill-challenge frameworks for each of these actions tailored to a four-PC, 5th-level table.
Hey! Im totally new to DnD and someone told me the campaign is called Qamis. What continent is Qamis on and who are its neighbors?

Welcome aboard! Short answer first: Qamis is the name of the continent on the world called Qaia. "Qamis" is the campaign-era continent you (probably) mean — the present-day setting after the Ryraxian War.

Neighbors and nearby lands (as written in the campaign notes):
- Westron: a whole other continent to the west. The notes say it exists but the DM hasn’t prepared much material for it yet — it’s large and described as “exotic.”
- The islands of the Central Sea: a chain of island states and pirate-ish island cities that sit inside the sea between continental shores. These islands are mostly thassalocracies of nautical merchant/mercenary families.
- A big island to the south: a largely unexplored, jungle-pressed island with a port city called Freeport on its shore. The island is mostly unexplored in the notes; some rumors say it might be where the dragons went, but that’s not detailed yet.
- The internal island-city states and isthmus: near the northern end of the Central Sea there’s a narrow wizard-made isthmus with two enormous bridges and a volcanic island; the city of Qaringil (important in the prequel-era campaign) sits on an island on the western side of that isthmus.

Also: there are a number of blank spaces on the map the DM hasn’t filled in yet, so some neighboring regions may be left intentionally vague for player backstories or future worldbuilding.
Cool. So is Qamis the whole world or just one continent? And is Qaringil part of Qamis?

Qamis is a single continent on the planet Qaia, not the whole world. The world is Qaia; the continent is Qamis.

Qaringil is not a separate world — it’s part of the same world (Qaia) but the term "Qaringil" is the name of a prequel campaign set about two hundred years earlier and based on an island city-state called Qaringil in the Central Sea. The political map is different in that earlier era: for example, the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r then, and some nations (like Rathakos and Acrokos) don’t exist yet. So when you hear Qaringil, think: same planet, same broad geography (islands in the Central Sea), but an earlier time with different political boundaries.
Oh neat. If Im starting in the present-day Qamis campaign, where do players usually begin? And how close are the other nations?

In the present-day Qamis campaign the party usually starts in Rathakos — a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains. Rathakos sits on the continent of Qamis and borders other Qamisi nations like Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos/Artklan regions, and several smaller polities (Acrokos, Lock, etc.).

How close other nations are depends on what you mean:
- Edrad, Artkos, Qis'mos, Forthyr and the smaller states (Rathakos, Acrokos, Lock, Kard) are all on the same continent of Qamis; many are immediate neighbors or regional neighbors across mountain ranges and rivers.
- The islands of the Central Sea and Qaringil lie off the coast inside that inland sea — not continents away but maritime neighbors.
- Westron is a whole other continent across ocean space to the west — definitely a larger-distance neighbor and described as exotic and not well developed in the notes.

If you want, I can point out which nations border Rathakos specifically, or outline the major nations on Qamis and their rough locations.
Yeah please — Im a total noob so maybe a quick list of the major nations and where they sit on the continent would help.

Sure — brief and newbie-friendly. All of these are nations on the continent Qamis (present-day campaign unless I say otherwise):

- Edrad: coastal, historically dominant, capital Jia on a peninsula into the Central Sea. Ruled by a half-elven nobility (Keyberos dynasty restored after the Ryraxian War).
- The Artklan League / Artkos: Artkos is the powerful northwestern state ruled by a High King; it includes exclaves (Ath'r is an important city) and the port/financial city of Kard in a southwestern enclave.
- Qis'mos: a rich agricultural heartland region in central Qamis; large Church of Bahamut presence; the holy city Qama is here.
- Forthyr: a big northeastern nation ruled by a sorcerer-king Dragoncaller Shanlong VI (dragon-calling dynasty).
- Rathakos: a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains where the campaign starts; it borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos and other small polities.
- Acrokos: another mountainous border region near Rathakos.
- Lock: a kingdom with an industrial bent; the city of Lock and a few cities are free cities.
- Kard: a major financial and shipping center (fragile governance; lots of Edran speakers there).
- Islands of the Central Sea: numerous island-states and merchant republics, often run by naval guilds and families. Qaringil (island city-state) is significant in the prequel era and exists in the same island chain in the present map too.
- The big southern island: mostly unexplored, home to Freeport at the shore.

Note: some geographic and political borders are intentionally left as "blank spaces" by the DM, so not every neighboring region is fully specified in the notes.
Sweet. One last dumb question: how do I say Qamis? And why is everything spelled with Qs?

Not dumb at all — good to ask. "Q" in these notes is pronounced "Kh" (this comes from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So Qamis is pronounced roughly like "Khamis" (a hard kh sound at the start). The setting uses Old Draconic spellings for a lot of place names and formal documents, which is why you see Q instead of a plain K in many names.

Anything else you want before your first session? I can give a one-paragraph scene-setting for Rathakos if you like.

Hey — quick skeptical question from a weekend-DM-tech-lead: the notes call Forthyr "somewhat backwards." That feels hand-wavey. Give me an executive summary grounded in the setting notes: what do we actually know, and what evidence supports calling it backwards?

Executive summary (facts from the campaign notes):

- Forthyr is an enormous nation in the northeast of Qamis (present-day campaign).  
- It is ruled by a sorcerer-king titled Dragoncaller Shanlong VI in the Qamis-era notes. (In the Qaringil prequel era the ruler is Shanlong I; keep eras distinct.)  
- The ruling dynasty used to be dragonborn, but "bloodlines ran thin" (i.e., dragonborn ancestry is now rare).  
- The notes explicitly say: "Forthyr is somewhat backwards," and that a prominent religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors receives tacit encouragement, if not support, from the Dragoncaller dynasty.  

What the notes do not say (important): they do not define in what ways Forthyr is "backwards" — social, technological, legal, religious, or otherwise. They also do not provide specific examples of institutions, industry, literacy, or infrastructure that would prove the label. That gap is a deliberate blank space the DM can fill.

Bottom line: the setting gives you three concrete anchors (location, ruler/dynasty history, and a politically significant reactionary sect) and a descriptive tag "somewhat backwards," but it does not supply the specifics that justify that tag. Any further detail is optional worldbuilding, not canonical.
Okay — so when people say "backwards" they could mean lots of things. From the notes, is there any indication whether that means low tech, reactionary religion, weak institutions, or something else?

Short answer: the notes point most directly to reactionary religion and political conservatism, but are silent on tech and institutions.

Why I say that (grounded in the notes):
- The only concrete explanatory detail attached to "somewhat backwards" is the presence of a prominent religious sect advocating the return of the Dragon Emperors, which the Dragoncaller dynasty tacitly encourages. That ties the phrase to religious/ideological backwardness or nostalgia for a previous theocratic/imperial order.  

What the notes do not state:
- No explicit statements about technology level, industrialization, literacy, roads, or bureaucratic competence in Forthyr.  
- No specifics about laws, trade policies, or whether the population is materially poorer than neighbors.  

So the safest reading from the canonical notes: "backwards" most concretely signals politically and religiously reactionary tendencies (Dragon Emperor nostalgia, tacit royal support). Any claim about infrastructure or tech would be extrapolation or a DM fill-in.
How does that political/religious backwardness affect neighbors or the recent war? Did Forthyr back Ryraxos or get involved?

What the notes say: 
- They do not state Forthyr's role in the Ryraxian War or any specific alliances or actions involving Ryraxos.  
- They also do not list diplomatic relations between Forthyr and neighboring nations in detail.

What you can reliably infer (and how to present it conservatively):
- The presence of a sect pushing for Dragon Emperors' return and tacit dynastic encouragement suggests Forthyr could be a source of ideological instability and regional tension. That is implied in the notes but not spelled out as historical fact.  
- Any claim that Forthyr supported Ryraxos, opposed him, or stayed neutral would be an invention beyond the notes. The campaign only notes the Ryraxian War and Ryraxos's exile; it does not attribute Forthyr a role.

If you need to portray regional effects at the table, treat them as plausible but non-canonical consequences: neighboring states might watch Forthyr warily, fear a theocratic revival, or compete to court/discredit the Dragoncaller. But again, those are DM-player-facing options, not stated facts in the notes.
Alright. Give me a one-sentence elevator pitch I can use in a session zero to explain Forthyr to players.

Forthyr is the vast northeastern kingdom ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI — a sorcerer-king whose dynasty once was dragonborn but now has thin dragonblood, described as "somewhat backwards" in the notes and home to a prominent sect that quietly pushes for the return of the Dragon Emperors.
If I want to illustrate "backwards" at the table without contradicting the notes, what are 5 quick, safe details I can drop into a session?

All of these stay within the notes (or are explicit, optional scene-building that do not contradict them):

1) Visible dragon imagery and ritual: dragons and imperial symbolism are everywhere — banners, carved reliefs, and public rites — reflecting the culture of Dragon Emperor nostalgia.  
2) Prominent clerical presence: a visible, politically active religious faction that preaches return of the old order and attends court, hinting at tacit royal favor.  
3) Sorcerer-king pageantry: the Dragoncaller dynasty rules with ceremonial magic and court ritual — sorcery is part of governance, not just private spellcasting.  
4) Signs of old aristocratic privilege: ritualized courts, slow-moving bureaucracy, and deference to noble lineage (which can feel "backwards" to cosmopolitan players).  
5) Thin dragonblood markers: nobles who claim draconic descent but with sparse and fading traits — a visual cue that the dynasty is old and its origin myth is fraying.

Note: the notes do not claim Forthyr is technologically primitive or materially poor. If you want to add those elements, treat them as optional worldbuilding choices rather than canonical facts.
hey ther — im a drow who stayed on the surface after the war. what are the dragon cults and where do they persist?
Do you mean present-day Qamis (the post-Ryraxian-war setting) or the Qaringil prequel era? A few details differ between the two.
present-day Qamis, please.

Canon from the Qamis notes:
- The text explicitly says some mountain communities in Rathakos “still follow the old dragon cults.” That is the clearest, specific location given.
- Forthyr (the large northeast nation ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI) contains a “prominent religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors,” and that sect receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty. That is described as a political/religious movement in Forthyr.
- Ryraxos, the dragon who led the Ryraxian wars, has been exiled to the second moon (Secundus) and “will play no role in the campaign.”

What the notes do NOT specify (blank spaces):
- No canonical account of the cults’ doctrines, rituals, hierarchy, or symbols.
- No named cult leaders, temples, or settlements beyond the general “mountain communities” in Rathakos and the vague sect in Forthyr.
- No explicit relationship in the notes between the drow enclaves (for example Qama) and the dragon cults.
- No description of how widespread the cults are elsewhere, or whether they are secret societies, open churches, guerrilla cells, etc.

So: canonically you have two clear leads — mountain dragon cult remnants in Rathakos and a pro-Dragon Emperors sect in Forthyr — and otherwise many blank spaces the DM has left open.
Are they hostile to surface races or specifically anti-drow? i want to know if i should hide or recruit among them.

The notes are silent on that. They do not describe the cults' attitudes toward specific races or give any record of anti-drow sentiment.

What is in the notes that bears on risk: Rathakos is politically unstable (internal unrest, contested southern reaches, mountain communities with old dragon cults). Forthyr has a politically charged sect calling for the return of Dragon Emperors and an active Dragoncaller dynasty—so involvement there is likely risky and political. Beyond that, you do not have canonical guidance on whether the cults target or welcome drow.

If you want a firm answer in play, this is a classic blank-space hook for the DM: either ask the DM whether the cults are xenophobic/anti-drow in your campaign, or use it as your character's opportunity to shape local attitudes (spy, convert, or try to subvert them). The notes explicitly encourage filling blank spaces with player backstory.
Any canonical ties between the Church of Bahamut and these dragon cults? Qama is a drow enclave, could there be tension?

The notes do mention the Church of Bahamut (big presence in Qis'mos; the holy city is Qama in the Qamis notes) but they do not describe any canonical interaction between the Church and the dragon cults. Specifically:
- Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence in Qis'mos and its traditional seat is in Qama (this is stated in the Qamis notes).
- The notes do not say whether the Church opposes, tolerates, infiltrates, or competes with dragon cults.

Separately: the notes also say drow were recruited as allies by Artkos and remain in scattered enclaves (most prominently Qama). But again, no canonical statement links drow enclaves and dragon cults.

So any tension or alliance between Bahamut's church, Qama's drow, and the mountain dragon cults is not defined in the setting notes and is a DM/player opportunity to create story details.
Okay. give me two quick, concrete player-facing hooks I can use to tie my drow to the cults — mark them as player-created since i know they're not in the notes.

Sure — these are explicitly player-created hooks (not in the canonical notes) you can bring to the DM:

1) Infiltrator-Confessor: Your character poses as a pilgrim to the Rathakos mountain cults, offering rare surface-goods and chants learned in Qama to gain trust. You secretly learn a few lines of their liturgy and a mountain shrine location. Use this to gather intelligence for Qama or to try converting a small cell to protect drow refugees.

2) Kin-as-Token: A distant relative of yours (surface drow) was raised among a Forthyr village sympathetic to the Dragon Emperor idea. That relative is now a low-level organizer in the sect and can introduce you, but the introduction binds you to perform a dangerous initiation task (a political kidnapping, a relic theft, or a speech at a local meeting) that entangles you with Forthyr politics.

Both hooks let you interact with the two canonical loci (Rathakos mountain communities and the Forthyr sect) while making clear these specifics are your backstory choices and not spelled out in the notes.

HIII — quick question: who is Exarch Giovanni Pesaro? I thought he was part of the Qamis present-day political scene, but I keep seeing references that make me think I'm mixing eras. I'm a DM looking to borrow him for a campaign — is that allowed?

Short correction first: Giovanni Pesaro is an NPC in the Qaringil prequel campaign (about 200 years before the Qamis present). He is NOT a present-day figure in the Qamis notes — using him as a living ruler in post-Ryraxian Qamis would be anachronistic unless you deliberately retcon or make him a namesake/ancestor.

What the notes do say about him (Qaringil era):
- He is the Exarch of Qaringil. 
- He has held the post for 19 years. 
- He is considered to be "on death's doorstep." 
- He is from a minor family but is considered aligned most closely with the Peixota and Manos families.

Context you can reuse from the notes (Qaringil city-state):
- Qaringil is a canal-crossed island city on a narrow wizard-made isthmus; population ~100,000. Primary language there is Islas (with heavier Qis vocabulary).
- The political life of the city revolves around seven Great Guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, Wool) and the Five Families. 
- The Five Families and relevant details: Peixota (Dolphin sigil) leads the Shipping Guild and is involved in mercantile/judicial affairs; Manos (Hand sigil) leads the Judicial Guild and is involved in silk/wool; Qanto (Dove) recently lost Mercantile leadership; Volmer (Crow) leads Banking; Vitelli (Moth) leads Silk. A gnomish family Tiddwar (Gears) is notable too.

What the notes do NOT say (blank spaces you can fill as a borrowing DM):
- Giovanni's policies, personality, precise illness or cause of death, family background beyond "minor family," specific alliances or favors he granted, or any deeds during his tenure. Those are open for you to invent.

If you want to borrow him, you have two clean options: use him as the Qaringil-era Exarch in a prequel-style campaign (staying faithful to the notes), or adapt the name/role into your own setting (for example as an elderly exarch, a namesake descendant, or a figure of local legend in your present-day setting). Which route do you want to take?

Nice — I want to keep his basic facts but adapt him into a present-day coastal republic in my homebrew. Any quick, safe suggestions for plot hooks or ways to expand his role without contradicting the notes?
