yeah do two quick festival ideas and a thieves' schedule tied to Tertius. keep them usable by a DM.

Sure — three tight hooks you can drop into play or hand to the DM.

1) Night of Three Lanterns (triple-full hook)
- When Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all near-full (rare, calendared events in the astronomy notes), coastal towns hang three lanterns at harbor mouths and hold all-night markets and songs of safe passage. For a thief: a triple-full night means more crowd cover but also more lantern patrols and public watchfulness. Use as a festival where pickpocketing is both easier in crowds and riskier because celebrations attract guard deployments.

2) Darkmouth Vigil (triple-new hook)
- During triple-new eras (all three inner moons near new), some inland villages hold a vigil of silence to "listen to the earth" — bonfires are banned and travelers mark themselves with ash to be recognized. For a rogue: triple-new nights are prime for shadow work (much darker than an ordinary new moon), but superstitions and volunteer watch groups may patrol roads.

3) Tertius Shift (thieves' schedule)
- Thieves' guilds and dock gangs structure shifts around Tertius's ~8-day period. A simple schedule: Day 0 (Tertius rise) — fence day; Day 2 — scouting; Day 4 — small lifts and courier intercepts; Day 6 — major score or heat-watch day; Day 8 resets. This rhythm keeps crews in sync with market cycles and with the predictable appearance of Tertius in the sky, which many gang members use as a timing cue for rendezvous. For your backstory: your guild taught you to read Tertius's phase as a cue for when the city is sleeping or when the docks are busiest.

All three are explicitly compatible with the moons and calendar data in the notes; exact dates, ritual details, and penalties/rewards are DM choices. Want me to write a two-line NPC quip for each hook you can paste into a backstory?
Hey — as an astronomy nerd building a PC, what does Luna (Earth's Moon) have to do with Qaia's seven moons? I keep seeing Luna used as a reference in the notes.

Good question — the campaign notes use Earth's Moon (called Luna) purely as a reference unit. MOONS.md defines Luna as 1.00 LD (lunar distance), 1 M_moon (lunar mass), and albedo 0.12. All the Qaia moon properties are given as ratios or comparisons to Luna so you can immediately grasp sizes, brightness, tides, etc.

What that buys you in practical terms:
- Distances and masses are expressed relative to Luna (so Secundus mass = 0.040 M_moon, Quartus = 1.000 M_moon, Primus = 0.0001 M_moon, and so on). 
- Apparent angular sizes are given relative to Luna (for instance Tertius is 1.09x Luna in apparent diameter; Quartus is 0.95x; Secundus ~0.90x; Primus ~0.34x). 
- Full-moon brightness and illumination are given as magnitudes and as fractions of Luna (e.g., Tertius full brightness = -12.60, which is 0.883 times Luna's full-moon illumination; Secundus = 0.407 Luna). 
- Tidal forces are normalized to Luna (Secundus tidal force = 1.47× Luna; Tertius = 2.04×; Quartus = 0.87×; Primus contributes a separate static bulge only).

So Luna itself is not in the sky of Qaia — it is a convenient Earth-based benchmark so you can immediately compare. The actual sky on Qaia is shaped by Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus (plus tiny Quintus at L4 as a worldbuilding footnote). Primus is geostationary and unique; Secundus is retrograde; Tertius and Quartus are large prograde moons and provide the familiar "big moon" experience.
Can you give me the quick, geeky rundown — angular sizes and brightness compared to Luna, and what that actually looks like in the sky at night?

Yep. Using the note-values directly:

Angular diameter (compare to Luna):
- Tertius: 33.7 arcminutes mean — 1.09× Luna (slightly larger in apparent size)
- Quartus: 29.6 arcminutes mean — 0.95× Luna (very close to Luna)
- Secundus: 28.0 arcminutes mean — 0.90× Luna (near Luna in size at mean distance)
- Primus: 10.4 arcminutes — 0.34× Luna (a small, steady disc fixed in the sky over the near hemisphere)
- Sextus: 4.1 arcminutes mean — 0.13× Luna (tiny disc)
- Septimus: 2.7 arcminutes mean — 0.09× Luna (very small)

Full-moon visual brightness (magnitudes and fraction of Luna):
- Luna (reference): magnitude about -12.74 (the documents imply this by the deltas they give)
- Quartus: -12.64 (0.908 × Luna illumination)
- Tertius: -12.60 (0.883 × Luna)
- Secundus: -11.76 (0.407 × Luna)
- Primus: -9.62 (0.056 × Luna — small and dim despite being close)
- Sextus: -8.02 (0.013 × Luna)
- Septimus: -7.14 (0.006 × Luna)

What that looks like, practically:
- Nights with Tertius or Quartus full will look very familiar to an Earth astronomer: one of those moons fills the sky at almost the same angular scale as Earth's Moon and produces nearly the same level of illumination. Tertius is slightly larger but marginally dimmer (albedo differences), so visually it is quite Moon-like. 
- Secundus is nearly lunar in angular size but darker; a full Secundus is bright (magnitude -11.8) but noticeably dimmer than a full Quartus/Tertius. 
- When Tertius + Quartus + Secundus are all near full at once (the system has triple-moon alignments several times a year), the combined illumination is substantial — you get roughly 2.2× the illumination of an Earth full moon if all three are fully illuminated together (0.883 + 0.908 + 0.407 ≈ 2.20 × Luna). That makes such nights far brighter than a normal Earth full moon night.
- Primus is visually small and dim (about one-third the apparent diameter of a Luna-like moon) but special because it never moves from its spot in the sky on the near hemisphere — it is a fixed, motionless marker. From the far side of Qaia you never see it.
- Sextus and Septimus are tiny bright points or small discs; they are noticeable to somebody looking for them, but they do not dominate the sky.

Motion/behavior to imagine:
- Primus hangs fixed above the equator at one longitude (the sub-Primus point). 
- Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are retrograde — they rise in the west and sweep east-to-west. 
- Tertius and Quartus are prograde, moving west-to-east. 
- The three big free moons produce complex, non-repeating tidal rhythms because their orbital periods are incommensurate.
You said combined illumination ~2.2x Luna for all three full. How would that translate visually? Would a triple-full be blinding? Any effect on night shadows or navigation?

Doing the straight photometric math from the notes: the three inner free moons at full contribute illumination fractions of roughly 0.883 (Tertius) + 0.908 (Quartus) + 0.407 (Secundus) = 2.198 times the illumination of Luna. In magnitude terms that combined light would be roughly 0.84 magnitudes brighter than Luna alone (because -2.5 log10(2.198) ≈ -0.84). If Luna's full-moon magnitude in the notes is about -12.74, the combined triple-full would be about magnitude -13.58.

What that feels like in play/description:
- Not "blinding" like the daytime Sun, but noticeably brighter than any single Earth full moon. Shadows would be stronger and crisper than on a typical full-moon night, and moonlit activities would be much easier. For example, reading large print outdoors, moving without torches, or sailing at night would all be simpler in a triple-full than on an Earth full moon night. 
- Because Secundus is significantly darker than the other two, some color/contrast will feel different — two bright, whitish moons (Quartus and Tertius) plus a darker, iron-rich Secundus give a layered quality to the light. 
- For navigation: Primus remains the fundamental longitude reference because it is geostationary. The brightness of the other moons doesn't change that. However, the much-brighter-than-normal nights during triple-fulls are a boon for visual navigation at sea (spotting hazards, reading charts, signaling), and they make stellar navigation harder because the sky background is brighter and fainter stars get washed out.

Notes and caution:
- The MOONS.md numbers are mean/typical values; exact combined brightness depends on exact distances and phases at the moment of alignment. The system also has eras where all-three-full alignments are common or rare on multi-year cycles (the notes give a triple-moon alignment calendar for 2253–2263 as examples).
Tell me about tides. I want my coastal PC background to reflect real tidal weirdness — how do the moons change the tides compared to Earth? Anything dramatic for harbors, estuaries, or sailing?

Tides on Qaia are more complicated and stronger than Earth's because multiple moons contribute appreciably. Key facts from the notes:

- The oscillating tidal load on Qaia from the freely-orbiting moons is about 4.4× the lunar tidal force on Earth. That comes from Secundus (1.47× Luna) + Tertius (2.04× Luna) + Quartus (0.87× Luna) ≈ 4.38× Luna. (Primus does not produce a daily oscillating tide.)
- Primus, because it is geostationary, raises a permanent static bulge of roughly 2 cm along the Primus–antiprimus axis. This is a fixed geographic offset of sea level rather than a periodic tide.
- The three oscillating drivers have incommensurate characteristic periods (note gives 9.85 h, 13.69 h, 12.43 h for the main oscillating contributions), so the resulting tidal signal is complex and non-repeating on short timescales. Expect irregular timing and amplitude of high/low water compared to the familiar twice-daily rhythm on Earth.
- The inner moons are almost certainly tidally locked, so their visible faces are stable, but that is separate from tidal forcing on Qaia.

Practical consequences for your character and coastal life (grounded in the notes, but left somewhat open for DM/you to flavor):
- Stronger peak tides: with a total oscillating tidal amplitude several times Earth's, some coastal currents and shallow-water flows will be more powerful than on Earth. This matters for small boats, ferries, and swimmers. Stronger rip currents and larger spring-neap modulation are plausible. The notes do not give exact coastal amplitudes (those depend on bathymetry), so you can decide how dramatic it is locally. The DM has left many shoreline details as blank spaces.
- Complex timing: harbor masters and pilots on Qaia cannot rely on a simple twice-daily schedule. Tidal highs and lows can shift in unpredictable ways because of beat patterns between the three moons. This makes local knowledge and charts (and experienced pilots) extremely valuable. Harbor pilotage is a real skill and a good flavor hook for a PC background.
- Triple-moon syzygies amplify or diminish tides: when the three big moons are aligned (all-new or all-full), their tidal forces add constructively. That can create exceptionally high spring tides or unusually low neap tides depending on geometry. The notes say the system cycles through full-moon eras and new-moon eras on a ~12-year cycle; those eras affect how often large constructive alignments occur.
- Secundus is retrograde and slowly spiraling inward on geological timescales; it doesn't suddenly change tides in the campaign span, but it's a long-term factor the lore notes. Primus being magically anchored means no secular drift from Primus' static bulge.

If you want a concrete PC detail: choose a home port where tide timing is a local art. Rather than saying "high tide every 6 hours," have your backstory mention "we only trust Old Mera's tide table; the moons mess everything up otherwise." That's fully supported: the notes explicitly call the tidal pattern complex and driven by multiple incommensurate drivers. The exact numeric tidal ranges at a specific harbor are a blank space the DM hasn't filled in, so you can invent the local numbers if you want.

Nice. Could you run the numbers quickly: if I want a descriptive line like "on triple-full nights the moons throw twice as much light as an Earth full moon and tides run wild, surging like..." — is "twice as much light" defensible? And can you give me one punchy sensory sentence about how a triple-full night smells/sounds at a busy harbor?

"Twice as much light" is defensible but a little conservative: the notes' numbers add to about 2.20× the illumination of an Earth full moon when Tertius, Quartus, and Secundus are all fully illuminated. So you can safely say "roughly twice as much light" or "a little more than twice as bright as an Earth full moon." Saying exactly "2.2 times" is also correct if you want precision.

Punchy sensory sentence for a PC backstory (grounded in the notes, but sensory phrasing is your invention):

- "On triple-full nights the three moons turned the harbor silver—more than twice the light of an Earth full moon—so ropes gleamed, gulls fell silent, and the tide hissed up the quay like a beast trying to climb stairs."

I kept the illumination claim honest ("more than twice the light") and tied it to the documented stronger, complex tides. If you want a version that's more technical or more poetic, tell me tone and I can tweak it.

Hey — I nerd out on celestial nav and I'm doing the primsine formula by hand while reading the setting. Quick two-part thing: 1) what cultural role does Jia play in Qamis? 2) I'm pretty sure Jia was the prime meridian in the older notes, but I think I mixed up Qaringil and Qamis eras. Also, can we sanity-check a primsine calc together? I started with the formula but want to make sure I'm not misremembering ρ or the steps.

Nice combo of worldbuilding and trigonometry. Two gentle corrections up front so we don't mix eras: "Qamis" is the present-day setting (post-Ryraxian War); "Qaringil" is the prequel about 200 years earlier. Jia is the capital of Edrad in both eras' notes, and it's described as the most culturally influential city in Qamis (present day) — a coastal peninsula city on the Central Sea. The notes do not assign Jia any specific longitude or say it sits at the sub-Primus (prime meridian) point, so if you thought Jia was automatically the prime meridian, that's an era/name mix-up or an assumption not stated in the documents.

Now the nav part: the primsine formula and the constant ρ are in NAVIGATION.md. ρ = R_Qaia / r_Primus = 6371 / 42160 = 0.1511. The primsine formula is exactly:

primsine(e) = ρ cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - ρ^2 cos^2(e)).

If you want, tell me the elevation e you were using and your latitude φ (or we can do a hypothetical). I can walk the arithmetic step-by-step and verify your result.

Okay good — I had read a line that made me think Jia was the prime meridian (maybe because it's such a cultural center), but glad you flagged that. No coords for Jia in the notes, got it.

Let's test with e = 50 degrees (I chose a round number). I want to compute primsine(50) by hand and then see how it would give me longitude if the city were at, say, latitude 20 N. Walk me through your arithmetic so I can compare to my notebook.

Perfect — do this by the same steps as the NAV doc. I'll show values to a few significant figures so you can compare.

Given: ρ = 0.1511, e = 50°.

1) compute cos(e) and cos^2(e):
   cos(50°) = 0.642787
   cos^2(50°) = 0.642787^2 = 0.413176 (approx)

2) ρ × cos^2(e):
   0.1511 × 0.413176 ≈ 0.06243

3) compute sin(e):
   sin(50°) = 0.766044

4) compute 1 − ρ^2 cos^2(e):
   ρ^2 = 0.1511^2 = 0.02283 (approx)
   ρ^2 × cos^2(e) = 0.02283 × 0.413176 ≈ 0.00943
   1 − 0.00943 = 0.99057
   sqrt(0.99057) ≈ 0.99527

5) sin(e) × sqrt(...) = 0.766044 × 0.99527 ≈ 0.76239

6) primsine(50°) = 0.06243 + 0.76239 = 0.82482 (approx)

That matches the expected form of the function; if your hand calc is within a few 1e-4 you're good. Now turn primsine into longitude magnitude for a hypothetical latitude φ = 20°:

cos(φ) = cos(20°) = 0.939693
cos λ = primsine(e) / cos φ = 0.82482 / 0.939693 = 0.87791
λ = arccos(0.87791) ≈ 28.6°

Finally: the sign (east/west) depends on Primus's azimuth in your sky. If Primus appears to the east of your local meridian, you are west of the prime meridian; if it appears to the west, you are east. So with the numbers above you'd be about 28.6° from the prime meridian (east or west determined by azimuth). Want me to re-run with your notebook values or a different latitude?

That's exactly what I did, nice — my sqrt step was the slow one so it's good to see your intermediate 0.99527 match. One other confusion: the NAV doc says the sub-Primus point is at 0N, 0E. Is there any rule in the notes that ties Jia to that point historically? Like, could the Keyberos dynasty have centered Jia on the prime meridian back in the prequel era and then later maps shifted? I'm trying to reconcile cultural centrality with celestial geography.

Short answer: the notes do not tie Jia to the sub-Primus point. NAVIGATION.md explicitly defines the sub-Primus point as fixed at latitude 0°, longitude 0° and treats that as the natural prime meridian because Primus is a permanent, motionless reference. But the campaign notes never assign Jia specific coordinates or say Jia lies at that sub-Primus point.

Longer context about Jia's cultural role (era-aware):
- In Qamis (present day): Jia is the coastal capital of Edrad and described as by far the most culturally influential city in Qamis. The Keyberos dynasty has been restored after the Ryraxian War and has reigned for four centuries historically (with a recent interregnum). The notes emphasize Jia's influence but do not give map coordinates or state it was the prime meridian.
- In Qaringil (the prequel, ~200 years earlier): the notes likewise present Edrad and Jia as politically and economically dominant; they say the Keyberos dynasty has reigned for two centuries in that era (which is consistent with the time difference). Still no explicit coordinate assignment for Jia.

So you can certainly make a historically plausible house rule that the Keyberos court chose Jia to be the practical reference for maps, or that ancient charts treated Jia's port as the map origin — but that would be a DM/party decision. The official docs treat the sub-Primus point as the objective, navigational prime meridian independent of any city. The map blanks are explicitly left open, so putting Jia on 0° is an option, not a stated fact.

Great, that clears it up. Last tiny check: the NAV.md example used e = 42.2° and got primsine = 0.7504 and then λ = 30° for φ = 30°. That's consistent with your numbers and method, right? I just want to make sure I'm matching the source examples when I practice.

Yes — that worked example is from NAVIGATION.md and matches the same formula and constant ρ = 0.1511. The doc walks through e = 42.2°, computes primsine(e) ≈ 0.7504, and with φ = 30° gives cos λ = 0.7504 / cos(30°) ≈ 0.8666, hence λ = 30°. Your method and my steps are consistent with that example.

If you want, I can generate a small table of primsine(e) at 5° increments so you can practice lookup-style nav (the NAV doc even suggests a 1° or 0.05° table). Or I can help you pick a plausible latitude/longitude for Jia if you want to place it at the sub-Primus point for a game — but that's a worldbuilding choice, not a canonical fact in the notes.

Hey — I'm building a character who's supposedly from Rathakos and grew up hearing stories of the Red Mountain Republic. I'm curious about Qaringil though: what are the "Great Guilds" there? Is the city like Waterdeep or the Forgotten Realms where a few guilds and secret societies run everything?

Quick clarifier before I dive in: the Great Guilds you asked about are from the Qaringil campaign (the prequel, roughly 200 years before the present-day Qamis campaign). In that era Rathakos and the Red Mountain Republic do not exist yet — the Holy Qis Empire rules much of the central continent. If you mean the Qaringil-era city-state, I can answer directly; if you meant present-day Qamis, tell me and I will switch focus.

Assuming you mean Qaringil (prequel): Qaringil is a canal-crossed island city-state built around commerce and maritime power. Its formal Great Guilds are: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. They are civic institutions and major centers of power in the city, not just trade clubs. The elites are commercial rather than landed, and the five great families dominate many guild posts (more on that in a second).
Okay yeah I meant Qaringil prequel — thanks for the pointer. How is that different from Forgotten Realms style guild dominance? Like do the Banking or Mercantile guilds secretly run the government?

Good — that keeps eras clean.

Short answer: Qaringil is oligarchic and mercantile, but its guild power is visible and institutional rather than an occult "secret society". Key differences to a typical Forgotten Realms comparison:

- Public, civic role: The Great Guilds in Qaringil are formal, public bodies (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, etc.) that control trade, law, navigation, and the bridges. They are the organs of governance and commerce rather than hidden conspiracies. The city-state is run by merchant elites and guild structures more than by mysterious masked lords.

- Family control: Four or five powerful families hold leadership across guilds. For example: Peixota (Dolphin) leads the Shipping Guild; Manos (Hand) leads the Judicial Guild and is deeply involved with silk and wool; Volmer (Crow) leads Banking; Vitelli (Moth) leads Silk; and a prominent gnomish family Tiddwar (Gears) leads Mercantile. That concentration of family influence makes politics commercial and dynastic rather than purely guild-versus-guild.

- Visible criminal underworld: There are criminal organizations that style themselves as guilds (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses), but they are described as competing underground groups, not the single all-powerful underdark syndicate. So there is criminal influence, but it is more fragmentary.

- Practical civic responsibilities: The Gondolier Guild controls bridge maintenance and the gondoliers — they are a major civic force, not just a transport union. The Judicial Guild handles city law, not a separate masked-magistrate model.

So think less "shadowy cabal that secretly rules the city" and more "merchant oligarchy with competing institutional guilds backed by old families," with a visible but fragmented criminal element.
Huh. So who actually runs the guilds? Are there named families like in FR noble houses? And is there a ruling council or a single Exarch like a lord?

Yes — the city is dominated by a small set of prominent families (the Five Families plus others) who lead or influence the Great Guilds. From the notes:

- Peixota: sigil Dolphin. Leads the Shipping Guild; also involved in mercantile and judicial affairs.
- Manos: sigil Hand. Leads the Judicial Guild; also involved in silk and wool. Their entry into wool is contested.
- Qanto: sigil Dove. Until recently led the Mercantile Guild; they still have roles in judicial and banking. They lost Mercantile leadership to a smaller gnomish family allied to the Exarch.
- Volmer: sigil Crow. Leads the Banking Guild; also involved in shipping.
- Vitelli: sigil Moth. Leads the Silk Guild; also involved in shipping and mercantile.
- Tiddwar: sigil Gears. A prominent gnomish family that now leads the Mercantile Guild.

As for city-level executive power: Qaringil is a republic ruled by an Exarch. In the current prequel-era notes the Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro, who has held the post for 19 years and is said to be on death's doorstep. The Exarch is supported and constrained by the guilds and the elite families — so power is shared and contested rather than monarchical. That is different from a single masked lord or a purely guild-run city in many FR portrayals.

Cool. My player brain wants to make a Rathakos-born character who studied maritime law in Qaringil and joined the Judicial Guild. Is that okay lore-wise? Also, how do the smaller guilds like Adventurer or Mage fit into this? FR has big wizard colleges that shape policy — is that true here?

Two important lore points to keep straight:

1) If your character is literally "Rathakos-born" and the timeline is the Qaringil prequel, that is an anachronism: Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist in the prequel era. The Holy Qis Empire rules much of the central lands then. If you want a character from the same geographic region as later Rathakos, you can pick a local polity or leave the origin blank — the notes explicitly allow blank spaces to be filled by player backstory.

2) Joining the Judicial Guild is perfectly plausible for a resident or an immigrant to Qaringil. Qaringil is mercantile and legal institutions are important. Also: the party being fifth level fits the note that "the party is fifth level so you can all easily be full guild members of anything relevant to your class." So mechanically and narratively it's fine.

About smaller guilds and mages: Qaringil lists smaller guilds relevant to adventurers — Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage — and says the party can be full members. That suggests magic-users and adventurers have formal guild presence, but the setting notes do not describe a single dominant wizard college that shapes city policy the way some Forgotten Realms institutions do. Mages are a recognized guild (so organized and visible), but the primary political power centers remain the commercial Great Guilds and the Five Families. If you want a wizardly cabal with political clout, that would be a player/DM choice — the notes leave blank how powerful the Mage Guild is relative to Banking or Judicial.

If you want a Rathakos-flavored origin without timeline trouble, options:
- Make the character from a frontier town that later becomes Rathakos (fill a blank space).
- Make them from a small settlement in the Red Mountains area (not yet organized as Rathakos).
- Or make them an Edran or islander who moved to Qaringil to study law.

Any of those lets you plausibly join the Judicial Guild.
Nice. So how pervasive is Islas language in Qaringil? And is Old Qis used in law like ancient tongues in FR? Also the pronunciation thing — how do people say Qaringil and Qis?

From the Qaringil notes:

- Primary language in Qaringil is Islas. The city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos, so the Islas spoken there has heavier-than-usual Qis vocabulary.
- Old Draconic (Old Qis) is the language of scholarly publications and treaties across the setting. So formal legal and diplomatic documents would often use Old Qis/Old Draconic, similar to how an ancient tongue appears in law in other settings.
- Pronunciation note in the setting: Q is pronounced "Kh" (from Old Draconic / Old Qis). So "Qaringil" is pronounced with an initial "Kh" sound, and "Qis" is "Khis" in pronunciation. The DM calls this out explicitly; if you ever need in-character dialogue, use the "Kh" approximation.
Last thing — if I lean on Forgotten Realms tropes for tone, is that going to clash? What vibes should I aim for if my char is a law-minded ex-frontiersman from the mountains?

You can borrow FR tropes for tone, but tweak them to the setting's specifics:

- Swap feudal/noble power for merchant-family oligarchy. Instead of dukes and lords calling the shots, think powerful shipping houses and guild heads. Your law-minded ex-frontiersman could be used to rough, personal justice at home and now has to learn bureaucratic, guild-driven legalism in Qaringil.

- Keep magic institutional but not necessarily dominant. Mages and alchemists are organized and visible, but they do not automatically outrank bankers or the Judicial Guild.

- Embrace maritime, canal-city flavor: gondoliers, bridges, and shipping law matter. The Gondolier Guild literally keeps bridges and canals functioning — that’s a kind of everyday political power unique to Qaringil.

- Use the blank spaces: the Red Mountains and a future Rathakos are not yet states in this era; your character's frontier background can be a useful contrast to Qaringil's tight-knit oligarchic elite.

Tone-wise, aim for commercial-politics intrigue and guild maneuvering with pockets of street-level crime and adventuring. That will keep your FR instincts helpful while making the character feel firmly Qaian.
How old is Ryraxos? I'm building a character from Rathakos (Red Mountain Republic background) and want local color about the dragon and lingering dragon-cult influence.

Quick era check: you mean the present-day Qamis campaign (post‑Ryraxian War)? If so, here's what's in the notes.

Age: Ryraxos is described as a bronze dragon, "relatively young (perhaps sixty)." The notes do not give a birthdate or exact age beyond that phrasing.

Role in the war (useful for local color): Ryraxos rescued Edrad from defeat early on and then spent about twenty‑five years waging aggressive war across the continent. The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years total. After his defeat he accepted exile to the second moon (Secundus) and "will play no role in the campaign." The notes also say he can shapeshift and that his rise began with service as an artillery commander—dragons "make excellent artillery officers, because they can help carry the guns." They also downplay his menace a bit: "less so than a competent adventuring party or a well‑commanded artillery battery."

If you want, I can turn that into a few Rathakos‑flavored attitudes and backstory hooks. Do you want suspicious townsfolk, secret cultists, veteran partisans, or something else?
Yes please — what would people in Rathakos think of Ryraxos and dragons more broadly? Also what are these "old dragon cults" in the mountains — are they tied to Bahamut or something else?

From the notes (present Qamis):

- Officially: Ryraxos is exiled to Secundus and "will play no role in the campaign." His record is complicated — delivered help to Edrad, then went on a long campaign. That's likely to create mixed memories: gratitude from some, trauma or anger from others.
- Rathakos specifics: the region is a mountainous border state with a strong frontier/adventuring tradition and recent unrest. The Southern Reaches were annexed from Edrad and there is internal unrest. Mountain communities "still follow the old dragon cults."

What the notes do NOT say: they do not specify how most townsfolk feel about dragons in general, nor do they define the doctrines, rituals, or origins of the "old dragon cults." They also do not state whether those cults are connected to the Church of Bahamut (Bahumat in the notes), which elsewhere is a major organized religion centered in Qis’mos.

So you have canonical anchors and intentional blank space:

- Anchors: Ryraxos = bronze, ~60, exiled to Secundus; Rathakos = frontier state, unrest, mountain dragon cults persist.
- Blank: popular sentiment and cult details.

If you want local color options for roleplay, pick any that fit the blank spaces. Examples (player/DM choices, not in the notes):
- Reverent pockets: some mountain villages still quietly venerate dragons as ancestral protectors; they keep shrines and chant old oaths. Good hook for a PC with dragon‑cult family rites.
- Bitter survivors: border towns that suffered under Ryraxos' campaigns may fear dragons and use dragon imagery as a curse or rallying cry against outsiders.
- Pragmatists: ex‑partisans who remember Ryraxos' early rescue of Edrad but hate what came after; they distrust dragons as unpredictable power.
- Secret cultists: small cells that secretly preserve draconic magic techniques and relics in caves — useful as an adventure seed.

Which of those fits your PC? I can convert one into a concrete family backstory, an NPC contact, and a starting tension in Rathakos.

I like the idea of my character coming from a mountain village that keeps a dragon shrine but is outwardly loyal to Rathakos' provisional government. Could you sketch a short backstory snippet and a couple of NPCs I could use?

Sure — keeping strictly to what the notes give and marking the rest as fillable flavor:

Canonical basis: Rathakos is a border mountainous state with a tradition of adventuring. Mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults. Rathakos is governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council looks for a monarch; the Southern Reaches are restless after annexation from Edrad.

Backstory snippet (player/DM content filling a blank):
- You were born in a high valley village in the Red Mountains. The village maintains a small, weathered shrine in a limestone cleft where offerings are made to an old dragon icon; the rites are more folk memory than organized religion. Publicly your village swears allegiance to Rathakos and pays taxes to the Regent’s collectors, but families still perform the shrine rites in secret or in small festivals.
- As a youth you learned both the frontier skills common to Rathakos (tracking, smithing, the language of trade caravans) and the hush‑kept rituals of the shrine — smoky incense, a handful of old draconic phrases, and a story about a bronze wyrm who once watched the valley. The family line claims a grandfather who fought in the era of the Red Mountain Republic against Edran occupation.
- You left to find work as an adventurer (Rathakos has a strong adventuring tradition) and now return with a few earned scars and a secret: you keep a shard of a strange bronze scale in a pouch under your cloak — a family relic from the shrine’s legend.

Two NPCs:
1) Maren Dun (village elder and shrine keeper) — elderly, pragmatic. Keeps the shrine tidy and will scold anyone who bores the village into conflict with Rathakos authorities. Loyal enough to the Regent to avoid open revolt, but protective of local rites. She can provide rumors and a favor: a small map to a cave the elders once sealed.

2) Captain Jory Vale (Rathakos militia officer stationed in the foothills) — young, resentful of the mountains’ secrecy. He enforces the Regent’s taxes and is suspicious of dragon‑rituals. He’s a possible antagonist or uneasy ally; if your PC is discreet about the shrine he might look the other way, but public displays invite interrogation.

All of the above beyond the canonical anchors is an optional fill to use at your table; the notes leave those mountain cult details unspecified so the DM can accept or change them. Want that scaled into 2–3 quick roleplay hooks (a secret quest, a militia checkpoint, an old rival)?

Yes, 3 quick hooks please. Also one last question: does the fact Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus affect myths about him — like is Secundus visible/important to folk? How might villagers mention the moon?

Three quick hooks:
1) The Sealed Cave: the elder gives you the map to a cave where the village once stored a "dragon relic." The seal is old and failing; whatever’s inside could attract militia, cultists, or treasure hunters.
2) Militia Checkpoint: Captain Jory sets up a checkpoint after rumors of a traveling draconic cultist. You must pass or divert him; a false move could turn a quiet village secret into a political incident with the Regent’s agents.
3) Rival Heirloom: a cousin who stayed behind claims the shard you carry is rightfully theirs and tries to expose your secret to curry favor with local powers (or to sell the shard to a mercenary from Kard).

About Secundus and folk belief (canonical facts plus sensible local color):
- Canon: Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon. Secundus is a real, visible moon: retrograde, about 0.9 times Luna in apparent size on average, darker (albedo 0.06), period ~4.53 days, and its tidal influence on Qaia is 1.47× Luna. Being exiled to Secundus is explicitly mentioned in the notes and is the reason Ryraxos "will play no role." 

- What the notes do not record: how common folk narrate that exile or whether they use Secundus as a symbol. That is blank space you can fill.

Suggested ways villagers might mention Secundus (flavor you can use freely):
- As a curse: "May you be sent to Secundus" — an exile’s fate. (Fits the canonical fact of exile.)
- As an omen: elders might say the moon’s retrograde path makes it a bad sign when it hangs low during certain rituals.
- As a sober reminder: old soldiers mutter that Ryraxos was "put on Secundus" and that the world is safer for it; others whisper that being sent there is not death but isolation.

All of those are acceptable table‑flavor; the notes give the astronomical facts about Secundus but leave cultural treatment to you and the DM. Want me to convert any of these hooks into a starter scene or a roleplay beat for session one?
yooo — I missed last session. quick catch-up: what's the population of Qaringil and what's its economy based on? need the lore bits I missed.

Just to confirm: you mean Qaringil in the Qaringil prequel campaign (the island city-state ~200 years earlier), right? If so, here’s the quick recap you asked for.

Population
- The notes give Qaringil a population of about 100,000.

Economy (what the notes actually say)
- Food: the city relies primarily on fishing and importation for food. That reliance shapes politics and vulnerability.
- Shipping and mercantile trade: Qaringil is a canal-crossed city with an armed merchant fleet. Its elites are commercial rather than landed, and the city’s power comes from shipping and trade.
- Guild system: the economy is organized around powerful guilds that manage and regulate trade and services. The seven major guilds are: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. Silk and Wool cover production and trade in those commodities.
- Banking and finance: the Banking Guild (led by the Volmer family) is a key player.
- Five Families: five prominent families control most major guilds and therefore political and economic power (Peixota = Shipping/Dolphin; Manos = Judicial/Hand; Qanto = Dove; Volmer = Banking/Crow; Vitelli = Silk/Moth). A prominent gnomish family, Tiddwar (Gears), is also important in the Mercantile Guild.
- Naval mercenaries: the wider islands in the central sea are populated by exiles and merchant-adventurers and run thassolocracies—armed merchant navies that sell services to continental powers, which feeds Qaringil’s shipping economy.
- Infrastructure: the city controls a narrow wizard-made isthmus with two enormous bridges; the Great Western Bridge is strategically important and dominated by Qaringil’s fortifications.
- Elites: the elites are primarily commercial (not landowners) and many now have landholdings elsewhere, but their power base is maritime commerce.
- Shadow economy: several underground criminal organizations operate like guilds (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses), so illicit trade and protection rackets exist.

Political context that affects the economy
- Exarch Giovanni Pesaro has been in charge for 19 years and is described as "on death's doorstep," which implies an impending power shuffle where guilds and families will jockey for influence.
- The city’s language and cultural mix: primary language Islas, with heavier Qis vocabulary due to historical Qismos settlers — this matters for trade links and diplomatic contacts.

What the notes do NOT specify (blank spaces)
- Exact export volumes, trade partners by name, tax rates, or detailed production numbers are not given. The hinterlands and precise supply chains beyond "silk, wool, shipping, and fishing/importation" are left as blank spaces for the DM or players to fill.

Want me to summarize how that affects PCs (guild membership, hooks, likely money flows), or do you want a one-paragraph "what you missed in last session" oriented to party events? I can tailor it to your character (class, guild interests, etc.).
Yeah, please: what are the practical takeaways for my PCs? like, what do we need to know about guilds, jobs, and opportunities? I'm playing a merchant-ish character.

Practical takeaways for merchant PCs (from the notes):

- Guild membership matters. The major guilds control commerce, shipping, finance, and legal authority. Being a guild member gives access to contracts, legal protections, and clients. The Adventurer/Alchemist/Mage guilds exist too and the notes say a fifth-level party can be full members of relevant guilds, so joining is realistic.

- Know which family controls which guilds. The Five Families effectively gatekeep major resources: Peixota (Shipping), Manos (Judicial), Volmer (Banking), Vitelli (Silk), Qanto (former Mercantile leader). Aligning with or offending a family changes what business opportunities are available.

- Shipping is central. If your character arranges freight, charters ships, or offers escort/insurance, that is a primary source of income. The city’s armed merchant fleets mean lots of private naval work, convoy contracts, and mercenary hire.

- Finance and credit are active. The Banking Guild (Volmer) matters for loans, letters of credit, and banking services. Merchants will need access to finance for large ventures.

- Commodities to trade: silk and wool are explicitly listed guild trades; fishing and imported food are critical imports. If you want trade routes or goods to traffic in, start with silk, wool, and general mercantile commodities tied to island trade.

- Political instability is opportunity and risk. Exarch Pesaro’s failing health suggests a coming shake-up: new appointments, shifting guild alliances, and potential contracts for parties who can influence outcomes.

- Bridges and canals are strategic assets. Control of gondoliers and bridge access influences movement of goods. The Gondolier guild (which, like the Wool guild, historically has not been run by a major family) is an independent lever you can exploit.

- Watch the shadow guilds. Thieves/Bandits/Cutpurses can be rivals, sources of black-market goods, or subcontractors for risky jobs.

- Practical hooks for a merchant PC: secure a banking line of credit from Volmer; broker a shipping contract with Peixota; offer naval escort to armed merchant fleets; undercut Qanto or the new Mercantile leadership; exploit the Gondolier guild’s control of bridge tolls; run contraband via island networks; or take a commission to sway a minor family during the Exarch succession.

If you tell me your character name, class, and whether you already have a guild affiliation, I can draft a 2–3 line roleplaying elevator pitch and a starter mission tailored to your build.

hii — quick question as a player: why cant a ship on the far side just use Primus to get longitude? seems like a lore loophole that makes navigation trivial everywhere. what stops them, and what should that mean at the table mechanically?

Good question — nice eye for a potential loophole. Short answer: geometry. Primus is geostationary over one hemisphere and is permanently below the horizon from the far side, so you simply cannot see it there. The navigation trick that removes the need for a chronometer (the primsine/elevation method) requires measuring Primus's elevation and azimuth, which is impossible if Primus never rises.

Key setting facts that explain this (from the moon/nav notes):
- Primus is fixed over the sub-Primus point at latitude 0°, longitude 0° (the prime meridian). It never moves in the sky for observers on the near hemisphere.
- Its orbital radius is 42,160 km and apparent diameter is 10.4 arcminutes; full magnitude about −9.6.
- The visibility limit is a geocentric angle of about 81.3°. Observers beyond that (the far-side hemisphere) have Primus permanently below the horizon.
- At high latitudes the longitude band that can see Primus narrows (example from the notes: at 60° latitude, only observers within roughly ±72° longitude can see it).

Because of that permanent geometric invisibility, far-side navigators must resort to the alternatives mentioned in the notes: dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, or lunar-distance methods using Secundus or Quartus. That situation is explicitly compared to Earth’s pre-chronometer longitude problem in the reference.
ok cool. so in-game: if my party is on the far side and the ship's navigator asks to make a check, how do you adjudicate that? are there canonical skill rules in the notes?

The notes do not specify mechanical skill DCs or specific skill rules — they describe the navigational reality and the recommended practical methods, but they leave exact game adjudication to the DM (blank spaces). That said, the setting guidance gives clear hooks you can turn into mechanics. A few DM-friendly options (explicitly house-rule suggestions, not canonical):

- If Primus is visible (near side): allow an automatic, high-quality longitude fix if the navigator has appropriate instruments and a clear sky (representing the primsine method). No chronometer needed.
- If Primus is not visible (far side): require navigation skill checks (Survival, Navigator's tools, or an Intelligence check with a navigator proficiency) to maintain an accurate course. Failure increases drift, travel time, or forces a stop to take stellar-lunar fixes.
- Lunar-distance method: allow a more difficult check (or a sequence of checks) to determine longitude using Secundus/Quartus against star catalogues. This mimics historical lunar-distance solutions and should require time and instruments.
- Dead reckoning: automatic but accumulates error over time; translate into a steadily increasing chance to be off-course unless routine checks succeed.

Use the notes where helpful: far-side navigation is explicitly called out as “substantially harder” and analogous to Earth’s pre-chronometer problem, so making it slower, riskier, and more skill-dependent fits the lore.
How about precision differences near the edge of Primus's visibility? Like, could you get a fix but at a penalty?

Yes — the notes call that out. When Primus is low on the horizon (near the visibility boundary) atmospheric refraction and shallow geometry reduce precision. The NAVIGATION.md gives practical guidance: precision falls off near the visibility limit, and high-latitude observers see a narrower longitude band.

So a sensible mechanical rule is: if Primus is visible but low (elevation near 0°), apply a penalty to the navigator’s roll or reduce the accuracy of the resulting fix (larger positional error). Conversely, when Primus is near overhead (high elevation), fixes are most sensitive and precise. Those fidelity rules mirror the stated sensitivity: best near the equator/prime meridian, worst near the edge of visibility.
Does this limitation apply in both the Qamis (post-Ryraxos) and Qaringil (prequel) eras? Or did something change?

It applies in both eras. The moon system (MOONS.md) and the NAVIGATION.md are shared across the Qamis and Qaringil campaigns, so Primus’s geostationary behavior and the visibility geometry are the same in the prequel and present-day eras. Nothing in the docs indicates that Primus’s orbit or the navigation method changes between the two timeframes.
Sweet. Last one: any neat roleplaying or plot hooks tied to this asymmetry?

Plenty of hooks built into the asymmetry — the notes emphasize it as a major practical and geopolitical feature. A few ideas grounded in the setting (all consistent with the docs):

- Near-side advantage: nations and ports on the Primus-visible hemisphere have a persistent navigational edge. That can shape trade, policing, and war — a campaign-level source of tension.
- Far-side isolation: far-side settlements could be suspicious of near-side navigators, depend on local pilots, or develop alternate star-chart traditions and costly lunar-distance almanacs.
- Smuggling/ambush: ships that know the far-side’s blind spots (no Primus fixes) could exploit navigational uncertainty to hide movements.
- Lost relics and blank maps: the notes call out many map “blank spaces.” A party operating on the far side could be sent to chart an unmapped coast where Primus is invisible — both a navigation challenge and a good adventure premise.

All of these are supported by the campaign notes’ explicit point that Primus creates a large asymmetry in navigational capability and that there are lots of blank spaces on the map to fill.

Isnt this setting way too complicated for a D&D campaign? Between the moons, the restored Keyberos dynasty, Ryraxos on the second moon, and that island stuff — I think I mixed up Qaringil and Qamis anyway. Help?

Youre right to feel overwhelmed, but some of the complexity comes from there being two related eras. Quick correction first: which era do you mean to run or read about? Qamis (the present-day setting after the Ryraxian War) or Qaringil (the prequel about 200 years earlier)? They share the same moon system and world, but politics and maps differ.
Oh! I thought Rathakos was in Qaringil and Giovanni Pesaro was the ruler after the war. Oops. Which things belong where?

Easy to conflate. Short era split:
- Qamis (present): Thirty-year Ryraxian War just ended. Ryraxos the dragon accepted exile to the second moon (Secundus). The Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad (capital Jia). The campaign is expected to start in Rathakos (mountain border state; Regent John Dunne is governing while a council picks a monarch). Many nations and postwar tensions matter here.
- Qaringil (prequel, ~200 years earlier): Takes place on the island city-state Qaringil (city of canals, canals, five families, guilds). The Holy Qis Empire rules the central continent from Ath'r. Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist in this era. Giovanni Pesaro is Exarch of Qaringil in the prequel, not a postwar ruler on the mainland.
If you tell me which era youre thinking of, I can tailor simplifications.
Im more a reader than a DM, but say I was gonna pitch this to a friend who might run it. How would you simplify it so its playable and not a lore lecture?

Good approach: pick a focused slice and treat the rest as background. Practical steps:
1) Choose one era and one start location. Example: Qamis -> start in Rathakos. Qaringil -> start in the city-state of Qaringil. Dont mix the eras in a single campaign.
2) Pick 2–3 active factions for immediate play. For Qamis a compact set could be: the restored Keyberos Edrad (capital Jia), the Artklan League/Artkos (High King Ezevir Arth), and the local Rathakos regency (Regent John Dunne). For Qaringil focus on the Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Volmer, Vitelli, Qanto) and the Great Guilds.
3) Use moon stuff as flavor, not a rules burden. The moon system is rich: Primus is geostationary (great flavor and a navigation trick), Tertius sets an 8-day week, Secundus is the moon Ryraxos was exiled to, and Quartus is the familiar monthly body. You can reference Primus as a cultural anchor or use the primsine navigation trick as a single optional mechanic — you dont need to simulate tides or precise orbital math at the table.
4) Simplify languages: follow the notes advice — national languages (Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas). Assume players speak the capital dialect plus one regional dialect; let them pick one extra language as background. Dont force detailed dialect politics unless its relevant.
5) Lean into blank spaces. The setting explicitly leaves many places undefined. Use those blanks for player backstories or to avoid committing the DM to a huge map.
6) Make a one-page cheat sheet: key NPCs, three local hooks, one map inset, and two cultural facts (example: Primus fixed point, Ryraxos exiled to Secundus). Thats enough to run multiple sessions.
That primus navigation thing sounds neat but intimidating. Could I just use it as a prop or small puzzle?

Absolutely. The primsine navigation method is perfect as a single-use puzzle or prop. The core facts you can use without heavy math:
- Primus is geostationary: a fixed point in the sky over the prime meridian. That makes it a unique landmark.
- Observers on the near hemisphere can use Primus to find longitude without a clock (in-world seafaring lore).
Use a simple tabletop puzzle: give players a measured elevation angle (or a crudely calibrated astrolabe), their known latitude, and have them consult a short primsine table to find their longitude. Or, more simply, have an NPC navigator declare "we're due west of the sub-Primus point" and let that be a clue. No need to teach them the primsine formula unless you want a math puzzle.
Any other quick tips for keeping the political stuff manageable?

Yes:
- Start small: focus on local issues (Rathakos regency instability, mountain enclaves following old dragon cults, or Qaringil family/guild rivalries) instead of continent-wide diplomacy.
- Use a small set of repeating factions (3–4) whose goals are easy to state: influence, trade control, independence, or restoration of old orders.
- Treat broad elements (Keyberos restoration, Ryraxian War aftermath, drow enclaves in Qama) as background color that informs NPC motives rather than daily mechanics.
- Use blank spaces as cliffnotes: if a player asks "whats east of X?" you can answer "thats a blank the DM will fill in with your backstory." That keeps the world scalable.
- If you want a longer arc, escalate from local (Rathakos) to regional (Artkos vs Edrad) once players are comfortable.

If you want, I can produce a one-page starter cheat sheet for Rathakos (Qamis) or a rival-family setup for Qaringil.
Yes please — a one-page Rathakos starter would be great.
Do you want that cheat sheet tuned to a reader summary (concise lore) or a DM starter (hooks, NPCs, factions, seeds)? Also confirm era: you mean Qamis (post-Ryraxian War)?

Hi — I play a character from Forthyr who follows the Dragon Emperor revival sect. Quick question for worldbuilding and strategy: what continent is Qamis on and who are its neighbors? I want to know realistic places to try to spread the sect.

Short answer: Qamis is the name of the continent on the planet Qaia. Its best-documented neighbors in the notes are
- Westron: a large, mostly unexplored continent to the west (briefly noted as “exotic” and not well detailed),
- the islands of the Central Sea: a ring of island states and thassalocracies that sell mercenary navies to continental powers, and
- a large, mostly unexplored southern island whose main shore city is Freeport (a city of exiles and pirates). 

Relevant local detail (present-day Qamis): Forthyr sits in the northeast of Qamis and is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI; the Dragon Emperor revival sect you follow receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty. The Central Sea has a narrow wizard-made isthmus and two enormous bridges near a volcanic island (this feature is in the notes). 

A couple of caveats: many border regions and the wider map have “blank spaces” the DM hasn’t filled in yet, and Westron is intentionally sketchy in the notes. If you want a political map or distances, those specifics aren’t provided.
Okay, can you clarify the religious landscape of those neighbors? Which places are Bahamut-friendly and which might be hostile to a Dragon-Emperor movement from Forthyr?

What the notes actually say about religion:
- Qis'mos (the central fertile region of Qamis) is strongly dominated by the Church of Bahumat; the holy city of Qama is the church's traditional seat. In the present-day notes Qama was occupied by drow during the war and there is now a schism (a drow-appointed high priest, Qe'lth IV, versus an alternate claimant Qachu II operating from Qis). That makes Qis'mos/Bahumat territory complicated, politically and ecclesiastically.
- Forthyr itself (your homeland) is explicitly described as having a prominent sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors; that movement receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty (present-day Qamis). So Forthyr is your natural base.
- Westron: the notes only say there are religious differences and that Westron is “mostly not being Bahumet worshipers” (this is a brief, underdeveloped note). Beyond that, the DM hasn’t specified how hostile or welcoming Westron would be to a Dragon-Emperor cult.
- The Central Sea island-states and Freeport: the notes describe them as commercial, thassalocratic, and law-of-the-sea oriented (exiles, pirates, mercenary navies). The notes do not specify their dominant religions; Freeport is mostly a haven for exiles and pirates and is described as trying not to look too hard at the jungle inland. Religion there is a blank space in the notes.

Bottom line: Qis'mos is the main Bahumat stronghold and therefore likely the most hostile to a rival dragon-revival ideology, though the schism in Qama complicates things. Westron and the islands are under-documented in the notes, so their attitudes are unspecified — good opportunities for character-driven expansion or for you to ask the DM to decide how receptive they are to the sect. If you want, I can sketch plausible local flashpoints the DM could adopt (ports to target, likely allies among island mercenaries, how to exploit the Qama schism) — say whether you want “canon-only” (stick strictly to the notes) or “DM-option” suggestions.

How was the Ryraxian War resolved? I'm a bit of a celestial-navigation nerd and I'm scribbling through the primsine formula by hand while I read — so if the notes say anything about moons or exile details, include those too.

Short summary (Qamis, present-day):
- The war lasted thirty years. It began when Qis’mos and the Artklan Empire moved to depose the Revolutionary Council in Edrad and restore the Keyberos dynasty. Bookmakers expected a short war; it did not end quickly.
- Ryraxos, a bronze dragon who first delivered Edrad from defeat, became a major war leader and then waged aggressive campaigns across the continent for about two and a half decades.
- The war ended with Ryraxos exiled to the second moon (Secundus). The Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad (they again rule Jia and have held power historically for centuries). Artkos’s supremacy over the central continent is described as restored.
- After the war things are politically unstable in other ways: the drow (recruited as allies by Artkos) remain on the surface in scattered enclaves (notably Qama), and many border or frontier regions (Rathakos, Forthyr, etc.) are unsettled.

What the notes do and do not say about the exile and the moons:
- The notes explicitly state Ryraxos was "safely exiled to our second moon" (Secundus) and as a result "will play no role in the campaign." That places the event firmly in the Qamis era (post-war).
- The notes do NOT describe the mechanism of exile (how they physically sent a dragon to a moon, what magic or ritual was used, who performed it). That is a blank space the DM has not filled in.

Relevant moon details (from the shared moon notes) you might care about when thinking of exile:
- Secundus: retrograde orbit, mass 0.040 M_moon, radius 471 km, density 6,689 kg/m^3 (iron-rich), mean angular diameter ~28.0 arcminutes (23.6'–34.4'), full-moon brightness about -11.76, orbital period 4.53 days, tidal force on Qaia = 1.47x Luna. The notes also say Secundus is iron-rich like Primus, suggesting similar composition.
- Primus (navigation anchor): geostationary, orbital radius 42,160 km, apparent diameter 10.4', magnitude -9.6, fixed above the equator at the prime meridian. Primus is the moon used for the primsine longitude method in the navigation notes.

If you want, I can walk the primsine calculation through step-by-step with numbers (rho = 0.1511) while you do your hand work. Want me to run the worked example from the navigation notes (e = 42.2 degrees, latitude 30 degrees)?

Yes please — the exile mechanism being blank is interesting. Now show the primsine arithmetic step-by-step for e = 42.2 degrees and latitude phi = 30 degrees, using rho = 0.1511. Do the intermediate steps (cos, cos^2, rho*cos^2, sin, the square root term) so I can check my hand math.

Good — brief note first: on the exile mechanism, the notes are silent. The DM left that as a blank space you can fill in. Now the primsine walk-through, matching the navigation reference exactly.

Constants and values:
- rho = R_Qaia / r_Primus = 0.1511
- elevation e = 42.2 degrees
- latitude phi = 30.0 degrees

Step 1: cos(e) and cos^2(e)
- cos(42.2) = 0.7408 (rounded)
- cos^2(42.2) = 0.7408^2 = 0.5488 (rounded)

Step 2: rho * cos^2(e)
- rho * cos^2 = 0.1511 * 0.5488 = 0.0829 (rounded)

Step 3: sin(e)
- sin(42.2) = 0.6717 (rounded)

Step 4: the sqrt term: sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e))
- rho^2 = 0.1511^2 = 0.02283 (approx)
- rho^2 * cos^2(e) = 0.02283 * 0.5488 = 0.01253 (approx)
- 1 - that = 1 - 0.01253 = 0.98747
- sqrt(0.98747) = 0.9937 (rounded)

Step 5: sin(e) * sqrt(...) = 0.6717 * 0.9937 = 0.6675 (rounded)

Step 6: primsine(e) = rho*cos^2(e) + sin(e)*sqrt(...) = 0.0829 + 0.6675 = 0.7504 (rounded)

Step 7: solve for longitude magnitude
- cos(phi) = cos(30.0) = 0.8660
- cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(phi) = 0.7504 / 0.8660 = 0.8666
- lambda = arccos(0.8666) = 30.0 degrees (rounded)

Step 8: sign (east or west)
- In the worked example Primus appeared slightly to the west, which by the rule in the navigation notes means the observer is east of the prime meridian. So the result is 30 degrees East.

Checks and notes:
- The intermediate roundings above match the navigation example in the notes and reproduce primsine = 0.7504 and lambda = 30 degrees.
- If you need more precision, keep more digits in each intermediate step (especially rho^2 and the sqrt). But for practical sextant work the given rounding is already sufficient.

Want me to re-run with more digits or a different elevation/latitude?
hai! when's the next great triple-new alignment? also i'm a bit obsessed with the Q/Kh thing in names — what's the deal with Q being pronounced "Kh" and Old Draconic?

Love the enthusiasm — two great questions! Short answer first, then the linguistics bit.

1) Next great triple-new alignment (from the notes):
- The moon reference table in MOONS.md / the triple-moon alignment calendar (2253–2263) lists the closest triple-new of that decade as May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT. The notes flag that one as the closest triple-new of the decade (all three inner moons — Secundus, Tertius, Quartus — within 0.1%).
- There are earlier triple-new entries in the same decade (e.g. 2256 Apr 26 and 2256 Sep 21 are both very close new-era alignments), but May 24, 2262 is highlighted as the standout triple-new.

A couple of important caveats from the docs: the alignment table covers 2253–2263 only; the notes do not give triple-alignment dates beyond 2263, so I can't point to the "next" great triple-new after 2263 from the provided material. Also the table times are listed in "PMT" but the notes do not define what PMT stands for, so if you need that time converted to a different clock or locale the docs don't supply the conversion.

Extra context that might interest you: the system cycles between "full-moon eras" and "new-moon eras" on a roughly 12-year rhythm — some years favor simultaneous fulls, others simultaneous news. Within a year the three inner moons come very close (within a few percent) multiple times; exact, best-of-decade timing is what the table gives.

2) Q/Kh and Old Draconic: concise, source-based answer from the notes
- The setting explicitly says Q is pronounced "Kh" (this comes from Old Draconic / Old Qis). Treat Q as a Kh sound when you say place names.
- Old Draconic (also called Old Qis in places) is the language of scholarly publications and treaties in the world — it is the orthographic and diplomatic source that leaves Q without a following u in many place names.

What the notes do not provide: a full phonological history or a step-by-step sound-change model. The materials give the pronunciation rule (Q -> "Kh") and identify Old Draconic as the source, but they do not spell out how or when the shift happened historically. If you want, we can:
- read the place-name list together and I can show how the "Q=Kh" rule applies (examples: Qamis, Qis, Qaringil — pronounce the initial Q as Kh), or
- draft a plausible historical phonology sketch consistent with the setting (I can do that, but it would be my extrapolation beyond the notes).

Which would you like — more triple-new dates from the table, or a guided tour of Q/Kh across the place names (or both)?

Oooh yes show me the names! i love saying them out loud. can you give me a short list of place-names from the notes with the Q pronounced as Kh? and maybe note which era (Qamis vs Qaringil) each name belongs to?

Heck yes, great to hear you want to say them aloud. I'll list names that appear in the notes and mark which campaign-era they belong to. Per the setting rule, pronounce the initial Q as "Kh".

From the Qamis (present-day) notes:
- Qamis (pronounce: Khamis) — the continent name for the present-day campaign.
- Qis (pronounce: Khis) — the language name and root of Old Qis; also region-name elements.
- Qama (pronounce: Khama) — holy city of Bahumat (mentioned in Qamis present-day notes).
- Qeth (pronounce: Kheth) — a region/city referenced as not controlled by Edrad in the prequel map notes but named in present-day notes too.
- Qirkos (pronounce: Khirkos) — a neighboring polity mentioned in the Rathakos context.

From the Qaringil (prequel, ~200 years earlier) notes:
- Qaringil (pronounce: Khar-ingil) — the island city-state where the prequel campaign is based.
- Qis (again: Khis) — the language/region; in the prequel notes the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r.
- Qamos / Qis'mos / Qis’mos (pronounce the same Kh- initial) — the name appears as Qis'mos in both eras' docs; treat as Khis-mos. (The prequel notes call it a region; the present-day notes describe a nation called Qis’mos.)

Cross-era notes and cautions:
- Old Draconic / Old Qis is explicitly the learned language and source of the Q orthography; the pronunciation note (Q = Kh) applies across eras because it is a pronunciation convention derived from Old Draconic.
- The docs do not provide a complete toponymic gazetteer, so there are many blank spaces; if you want more named examples I can pull every Q-starting name that appears in the notes.

Want me to read them out in a little phonetic-friendly spelling for you to practice (e.g., "Qaringil = KHAH-ring-il"), or would you rather I keep strictly to the "Q = Kh" rule and give no extra phonetic cues?
hi! tell me about Tertius and why it defines an 8-day week — i'm a tech lead who plays D&D on weekends and want an executive summary of the world (short, punchy, excited)

Nice — love the detail-focus. Executive summary, punchy and number-rich:

- What Tertius is: one of Qaia's major moons (shared across both the Qamis present-day and the Qaringil prequel — the moon system is the same in both eras).

- Key physicals (short): mass 0.180 M_moon, radius 839 km, density 5,351 kg/m^3 (iron-enriched), albedo 0.09, apparent angular diameter mean 33.7 arcminutes (about 1.09x Earths Moon), full-moon brightness -12.60 (very nearly as bright as Quartus/"Luna").

- Dynamics that matter: Tertius is prograde with an orbital period of 8.15 days. That 8.15-day orbital period is explicitly the basis for Qaia's week — i.e., people take the moon's ~8-day cycle as the natural cadence for a week.

- Why that matters culturally/physically (what the notes say): because Tertius' period gives a persistent, observable rhythm in the sky, it became the natural anchor for the local week. It also contributes a large tidal effect (tidal force ~2.04× Luna), so it shapes daily coastal rhythms and navigation considerations.

- Sky feel: slightly larger than Quartus in the sky but a touch darker; Tertius and Quartus move west-to-east across the sky (prograde), while Secundus is retrograde. Primus is geostationary and fixed over the equator.

- What the notes do not say: the setting notes do not spell out how civil calendars reconcile the 8.15-day period (rounding rules, intercalary days, naming of weekdays, etc.). Those calendar details are blank and left for the DM/players to define.

Want a one-line blurb you can drop in a session pitch or a tiny "cheat sheet" card for players?
Yes — give me that one-line blurb and a tiny cheat-sheet card (one paragraph) that I can paste into a session handout.

One-line blurb:
Tertius is Qaia's iron-enriched prograde moon with an 8.15-day orbit — its steady ~8-day cycle is the cultural basis for Qaia's week and a dominant driver of tides.

Cheat-sheet paragraph (session handout):
Tertius — the "eight-day moon": a dense, iron‑enriched prograde moon (mass 0.180 M_moon, radius 839 km) that appears slightly larger than Quartus in the sky (mean diameter 33.7'). Its orbital period is 8.15 days, and that rhythm is the basis for Qaia's week. Tertius is a major tidal driver (tidal force ≈2.04× Luna), so coastal schedules and some festivals track its cycle. Note: the setting notes state Tertius defines the week but do not specify how civil calendars handle the 0.15-day fraction — that detail is left open for table/worldbuilding.

Want this in a compact plaintext card format (short lines) I can paste into chat or a doc?
Nice. Quick follow-up: does Tertius' 8.15-day period mean people use 8.15-day weeks literally, or do they round? Any canonical answer in the notes?

Short answer: the notes only give one canonical fact — Tertius' period (~8.15 days) is the basis for Qaia's week. They do not spell out whether calendars use 8.15-day weeks, round to 8 days, insert intercalary rules, or how weekday names work. That's an intentional blank space in the docs for the DM/players to decide.

If you want a canonical option to use at your table, two safe choices are:
- Round to an 8-day week (simple, fits play schedules); or
- Treat the week as "8-day nominal" with occasional festival/intercalary adjustments to absorb the 0.15-day drift (gives you a regular worldbuilding hook).

I can draft a canonical-sounding rounding rule and weekday names that stay faithful to the notes if you want — but I won't invent that unless you ask.
Do it — give me a simple canonical rule and suggested weekday names (one sentence each) I can drop into a campaign pack. Keep it consistent with the notes and don't invent history.

Got it — consistent with the notes (no invented history), minimal invention that treats the blank as a practical rule:

Canonical rule (minimal, practical): Calendars use an 8-day nominal week tied to Tertius' cycle; every 40 weeks a single intercalary day is added to keep civil time roughly aligned with the moon (this is a practical table rule and is not stated in the setting notes but provided only as a play-friendly option). If you prefer strictly canonical-to-notes: the notes only assert the 8.15-day orbital period underpins the week and leave any rounding or intercalation unspecified.

Suggested weekday names (short, neutral; one sentence each):
- Primday: start of the nominal week, named for the fixed-sky anchor Primus (short, bright reference). 
- Tersday: the day aligned with Tertius' rising cycle — the cultural midweek anchor. 
- Merday: common market and mid-cycle business day. 
- Forthday: a working day used in many coastal towns. 
- Seaday: for sailors and islanders, often a flexible rest or working day. 
- Highday: commonly chosen for religious observances and council meetings. 
- Restday: widespread day-off customs in many cities. 
- Moonset: nominal end of the week; festivals often occur here before the intercalary adjustment.

Reminder: the notes explicitly leave calendar specifics blank; these names and the "every-40-weeks" intercalary suggestion are play-tools you can adopt — they are not present in the DM's source notes unless you choose to use them. Want these as a printable one-liner per day for a handout?
Perfect. Yes, paste the one-liner per day list in plain text I can paste into a doc.

Primday — start of the nominal week, named for the fixed-sky anchor Primus.
Tersday — day aligned with Tertius' cycle; cultural midweek anchor.
Merday — market and mid-cycle business day.
Forthday — common working day in coastal towns.
Seaday — sailors' and islanders' flexible rest/work day.
Highday — often used for religious observances and council meetings.
Restday — widespread day-off customs in many cities.
Moonset — nominal end of the week; frequent time for festivals before intercalary adjustments.

If you want, I can also output this as a tiny printable card (fixed-width plain text) or give a one-line rationale you can paste into your campaign notes explaining the intercalary rule.
morning! I'm building a half-elven Edran noble PC and digging into the dynastic context. What exactly are these "thassolocracies" in the notes, and which places in Qamis actually qualify?

Short answer from the notes: a thassolocracy in this setting is a sea-power polity dominated by maritime "robber barons" who control strong mercenary navies and sell those fleets' services to continental powers. The campaign notes name the islands of the central sea specifically as thassolocracies in both eras (the Qaringil prequel and the Qamis present).

Key points you can rely on (explicit in the docs):
- "Thassolocracy" here = island-based maritime states run by nautical robber barons, whose power is essentially their mercenary navies. They sell those navies to continental powers.
- In the Qaringil-era notes: many of the islands of the central sea are described as thassolocracies; Qaringil itself is an island city-state (an "incomprehensible republic") with armed merchant fleets, but it is not explicitly labeled a thassolocracy in the notes.
- In the Qamis (present-day) notes: the islands of the central sea are again described as a thassolocracy of nautical robber barons. Qaringil (in the prequel notes) is emphasized as a powerful merchant/republic city with armed fleets and powerful guilds, not as a robber-baron thassolocracy per se.

What the notes do not say (blank spaces):
- No named list of the individual island polities beyond Qaringil and the general description — the internal politics of those thassalocracies are left intentionally vague.
- No explicit, canonical relationship spelled out between Edrad/Keyberos dynasty and the island thassalocracies.

For an Edran half-elven noble PC: the notes give you a solid, canonical hook (islands sell mercenary navies to continental powers) but leave the exact dynastic ties, treaties, or patronage arrangements unspecified. You can reasonably write your noble as having mercantile or patronage ties to one or more island fleets, and that will fit the setting — but those specifics are DM/PC backstory choices because the docs mark the islands' finer details as blank spaces.
Okay — so is Qaringil itself a thassolocracy, or is it a different model? And would the Keyberos court realistically lean on island navies the way Artkos or Kard might?

Qaringil vs. the thassalocracies: the notes distinguish them.
- Qaringil (prequel era) is described as an island city-state and "an incomprehensible republic" whose elites are commercial and whose armed merchant fleets and fortifications dominate the nearby bridge. It is not explicitly called a thassolocracy in the notes; instead it looks like a powerful mercantile republic whose power derives from trade and organised armed fleets.
- The phrase "thassolocracies of nautical robber barons" in the notes is applied to the islands of the central sea more generally. So many island polities around the central sea are thassalocracies; Qaringil is an important island city-state in the same sea but described differently (republical, guild-dominated).

About the Keyberos court using island navies: the notes do not explicitly state whether Edrad or the Keyberos dynasty hires or depends on island mercenary fleets. What the notes do state is:
- The islands sell their "powerful mercenary navies to continental powers," which plausibly includes any of the major continental states (Edrad, Artkos, Qis'mos, etc.), but the document does not name particular client relationships.
- Edrad is historically the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis and ruled by the Keyberos dynasty from Jia. That dominance makes it reasonable in-universe that Edran nobles or the court could be major clients or competitors for island fleets, but that is not spelled out by the DM notes.

If you want a canonical-safe approach for your PC:
- Treat the central-sea islands as sellers of mercenary navies (explicit canon). Your PC could plausibly have family contracts, trading interests, or patronage ties to one of those fleets.
- If you want a named ally or rival on the islands, note that the DM left most island polities blank — that is an intended space to build your house-level relationship (and to pitch to the DM). Qaringil itself is a clear, pre-existing island power (a republic with guilds and armed merchant fleets) you could plausibly interact with, but it is not labeled a thassalocracy in the notes.

If you want, I can suggest a few constrained, setting-faithful hooks you can propose to the DM for your noble's dynastic ties (explicitly flagged as player/DM choices, not canonical facts).
